figures.name: Saeed Ahmed Lootah
figures.title: Founder and Developer
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Port Saeed locality in Dubai
figures.significance: Established Port Saeed in Dubai, naming it after the Egyptian city of Port Said.
figures.biography:
Saeed Ahmed Lootah was a prominent Emirati developer and businessman who established the Port Saeed locality in Dubai. He named this development after the Egyptian city of Port Said, which had been destroyed during the Anglo-French invasion in the Suez Crisis, demonstrating his awareness of contemporary Arab political events. Port Saeed became an important area in Dubai, strategically located along Dubai Creek and bordered by Al Khabisi to the north, Al Garhoud to the east, and Rigga Al Buteen to the west.

The locality he founded stretches between Al Garhoud Bridge and Al Maktoum Bridge and includes significant landmarks such as Deira City Centre and Dubai Creek Golf and Yacht Club. His development featured important infrastructure including a marina, golf course, and various hospitality facilities. The area has continued to evolve, with Dubai Municipality redesigning parts of the residential neighborhood after 2003, resulting in several new buildings being constructed in the area he originally established.
achievements: Founded Port Saeed locality in Dubai
Created strategic waterfront development along Dubai Creek

figures.name: Ali Lootah
figures.title: Chairman
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of Nakheel Properties
figures.significance: Served as Chairman of Nakheel, overseeing major Dubai development projects including Palm Jebel Ali.
figures.biography:
Ali Lootah served as Chairman of Nakheel, one of Dubai's major property development companies responsible for iconic projects including the Palm Islands. During his tenure, he oversaw the complex challenges facing the Palm Jebel Ali project, which had been stalled due to the 2008 financial crisis. In March 2015, he publicly confirmed that Nakheel remained committed to the Palm Jebel Ali project in the long term, though he acknowledged the difficulties facing original investors by asking "what can I do" for them.

Lootah's leadership came during a challenging period for Dubai's real estate sector, as the company dealt with the aftermath of the global financial crisis and its impact on large-scale development projects. Under his chairmanship, Nakheel had to navigate complex investor relations, particularly regarding the stalled Palm Jebel Ali development, while maintaining the company's commitment to completing its major projects. His tenure represented efforts to sustain Dubai's ambitious development vision despite significant economic headwinds.
achievements: Led Nakheel during challenging post-2008 financial crisis period
Maintained long-term commitment to major Dubai development projects

figures.name: Kazim al-Samawi
figures.title: Iraqi Poet and Journalist
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Iraqi poet and journalist known for his humanist worldview and exile writings
figures.significance: Known as 'The Elder of Iraqi exiles' and was a leading leftist poet who spent over half his life in political exile.
figures.biography:
Kazim Jasir Faraj, better known as Kazim al-Samawi, was born in 1925 in Samawah, Iraq. He graduated from the Rural Teachers' House in 1940 and began his literary career in the late 1940s. He rose to prominence as a left-wing realist poet and committed journalist, founding the newspaper Al-Insaniyah (The Humanity) in 1956, though it was closed in 1961.

Al-Samawi spent more than half of his life in exile as a political refugee, moving between Lebanon, Hungary, Germany, China, Syria, Cyprus, and finally Sweden. His Iraqi citizenship was revoked by Nuri al-Said's government in 1954, forcing him to seek asylum in Hungary. He witnessed the death of nearly all his family members during his years of exile, including his sons Nasir (assassinated in Beijing in 1991) and Riyad (died of cancer), his daughter Tahrir (died in London in 2008), and his wife.

He was involved in the World Peace movement and contributed to establishing the peace movement in Iraq in 1952. Al-Samawi died on 15 March 2010 in Stockholm, Sweden, at age 85. President Jalal Talabani ordered his body to be transferred to Iraq by private plane, and he was buried in Sulaymaniyah's Saiwan Cemetery as per his wishes, since he was an advocate of Iraqi Kurdish nationalism.
achievements: Founded the newspaper Al-Insaniyah (The Humanity) in 1956
Published seven poetry collections translated into several languages
Contributed to establishing the peace movement in Iraq in 1952
Represented the Arab world at the Asia and Pacific Rim Peace Conference in Beijing in 1952
Member of the Hungarian Writers' Union

figures.name: Ayla Halit Kazım
figures.title: Turkish Cypriot Politician
figures.roleDescription: First female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus
figures.significance: She was the first female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus, serving briefly in the legislature in 1963.
figures.biography:
Ayla Halit Kazım was born in Larnaca in 1934, daughter of Mehmet Zeki Müderisoğlu from Larnaca and Nezihe Efendi from Limassol. She grew up in Limassol and attended the American Academy in Larnaca. In 1953 she married Shemshedin Halit Kazım and became a farmer, settling in Paphos where they had four children.

Her husband Shemshedin became the Turkish mayor of Paphos and was elected to the House of Representatives in 1960. However, he died in a car accident in September 1963. Ayla was chosen to replace him and was elected without a by-election, taking office as the first female member on 25 October 1963. She joined the Independent Turkish Group, but alongside the other 14 Turkish Cypriot members, withdrew from the House in December 1963.

Following the 1974 coup and Turkish invasion, Kazım moved to what became Northern Cyprus, settling in Güzelyurt. She subsequently became a member of the Community Assembly of the Autonomous Turkish Cypriot Administration. In 2006 she won a court case entitling her to compensation from the Cypriot government, which had expropriated land she owned in Mandria.
achievements: First female member of the House of Representatives of Cyprus
Member of the Community Assembly of the Autonomous Turkish Cypriot Administration
Won court case for land compensation in 2006

figures.name: Ibrahim Galadari
figures.title: Merchant and Father of the Galadari Brothers
figures.roleDescription: Second-generation patriarch who continued the family business
figures.significance: Father of the three Galadari brothers who founded the modern Galadari Group conglomerate and played a key role in Dubai's economic development.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim Galadari was the son of merchant Hassan Galadari and father to the three brothers who would establish the Galadari Brothers Group. Also known as Ebrahim Galadari, he continued the family's merchant tradition established by his father in the late 19th century. Ibrahim raised his sons in Iran, where Abdul Latif Galadari was born in 1939.

Ibrahim's role was crucial in maintaining the family's commercial legacy between the founding generation of Hassan Galadari and the expansion generation of his three sons: Abdul Rahim, Abdul Latif, and Abdul Wahab. His sons would go on to establish one of the UAE's most successful business conglomerates in the early 1960s.

figures.name: Abdul Rahim E. Galadari
figures.title: Co-founder of Galadari Brothers Group
figures.roleDescription: Elder brother and co-founder of the Galadari business empire
figures.significance: Co-founded the Galadari Brothers Group in the early 1960s and helped build one of the UAE's largest business conglomerates.
figures.biography:
Abdul Rahim E. Galadari was the elder brother of Abdul Latif Galadari and co-founder of the Galadari Brothers Group, established in the early 1960s. Along with his younger brothers Abdul Latif and Abdul Wahab, he built what would become one of the UAE's most successful business conglomerates. The brothers were involved in the gold business, which made them multimillionaires by the 1970s.

Abdul Rahim and his brothers were well-positioned to take advantage of Dubai's 1970s oil boom, establishing major enterprises including the Intercontinental Hotel, Dubai Bank, a Mazda vehicle dealership, the Khaleej Times newspaper, and numerous construction, engineering, and trading companies. His son Mohammed Abdul Rahim Galadari attended Abdul Latif's funeral in 2002, representing the continuing family legacy.
achievements: Co-founded Galadari Brothers Group in early 1960s
Built multimillion-dollar gold business by 1970s
Established major hotels, banks, and media companies

figures.biography:
Abdul Wahab E. Galadari was one of the three founding brothers of the Galadari Brothers Group, established in the early 1960s alongside his brothers Abdul Rahim and Abdul Latif. The three brothers initially worked together to build their business empire, starting with the gold trade that made them multimillionaires by the 1970s.

In 1976, the brothers underwent a major restructuring when Abdul Wahab was bought out by Abdul Rahim and Abdul Latif, who retained control of the business. This split allowed the remaining two brothers to continue developing what would become one of the UAE's largest conglomerates, while Abdul Wahab pursued separate business interests.
achievements: Co-founded Galadari Brothers Group in early 1960s

He attended the funeral of his uncle Abdul Latif Galadari in Dubai in March 2002, demonstrating the continued close family ties and his role as a representative of the younger generation.

As a member of the third generation, Mohammed Abdul Rahim Galadari represents the continuation of the family legacy established by his grandfather Ibrahim and great-grandfather Hassan Galadari in the 19th century.

Ahmad Bin Shafar was appointed to lead a new administration for Al Wasl Football Club for the 2021-22 season. His appointment came after a period of poor results that had led to mutual bad feeling between the club and its fans, with supporters campaigning for a change in the administration and board of directors.

Under his leadership, the club saw significant improvements in both fan relations and team performance. With the fans placated by the new administration, attendances rose, and the team's spirit and performance improved, though they only achieved 6th place in the league that season. His tenure marked the beginning of a turnaround for the club that would eventually lead to their most successful period in years.

figures.name: Mohammad Abdallah Al Gaz
figures.title: Former Chairman of the UAE Central Bank
figures.roleDescription: Financier, developer, diplomat, central banker and philanthropist
figures.significance: A pioneering figure in UAE financial development and philanthropy who helped establish the country's first charitable institution.
figures.biography:
Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz was born in Al Dhagaya, Deira in 1920 to a modest family. His father, Abdulla Mohamed AlFalasi, died unexpectedly when AlGaz was an adolescent, but his mother enrolled him in the Al Ahmadiya School. During World War II famine, he temporarily settled in Bahrain with his family while working as a carpenter in Dammam, using his earnings to start a commodity trading operation.

Emerging as an enterprising trader in textiles and commodities, AlGaz entered the historic Switzerland-Dubai-India gold trade in the 1950s after partnering with Juma Al Majid to establish the famous Mohamed & Juma Al Majid company. He rose to political power as a trusted advisor of Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed al Maktoum and was nominated to the Dubai Municipal Council on July 7, 1958. After the gold trade declined, he and Al Majid secured franchise rights for international brands including General Electric, Samsung, Phillips, Opel, Pepsi, Nissan, Hyundai, and Kia before splitting their assets amicably after 11 years.

Post-federation, AlGaz served as the first Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from 1980 into the mid-1990s under Chairman Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan. He was instrumental in UAE's economic development and philanthropy, building schools including the Amna bint Wahb Girls School and Gamal Abdulnasser Boys School. In 1989, he developed with Juma Al Majid the UAE's first charitable society, Beit Al Khair. He died at age 96 on April 12, 2016, at Cleveland Clinic Abu Dhabi.
achievements: First Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE (1980-1990s)
Co-founder of Beit Al Khair, UAE's first charitable society (1989)

figures.biography:
Mohammed Farooq, known as The Great Farooq, was the son of Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil. After his father died, Mohammed Farooq took over as the head of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones. Today Mohammed Farooq is remembered as the most famous pearl merchant of Lengeh, Bastak and the Persian Gulf.

As Coles and Jakson put it, 'The Great Farooq's generosity and hospitality earned him respect and a reputable status amongst Gulf royalty.' He had close ties with the rulers of Arab States of the Persian Gulf, namely the late Sheikh Mohammed Bin Saqer Al Qasimi, the former ruler of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah and the late Sheikh Saeed Bin Maktoum Al Maktoum, The former Ruler of Dubai. His daughter Faiqa Farooq became the Arab world's first female ambassador of the United Nations.
achievements: Most famous pearl merchant of the Persian Gulf

Ahmed Al Arshi was the son of Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil and brother to Mohammed Farooq (The Great Farooq). After their father Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil died, his sons Mohammed Farooq and Ahmed Al Arshi took over as the heads of the family expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones.

Ahmed Al Arshi worked alongside his more famous brother in building the family's international trading empire. The family branches are closely related, with the Farooq Al Arshi family being first cousins of the Al Olema family, also known as the Sheikh Sultan Al Ulama, the Fikree, and the Al Khaldi family.

figures.roleDescription: Emirati poet, artist, and cultural advocate
figures.significance: Award-winning Emirati poet and artist, Harvard graduate, and cultural ambassador who won the ADMAF Creativity Award for her poetry collection.
figures.biography:
Shamma Al Bastaki was born in Dubai in 1996 and is an Emirati poet and artist. She holds a master's degree from Harvard University and a bachelor's degree from New York University Abu Dhabi, where she double majored in Social Research and Public Policy as well as Literature and Creative Writing. She completed diplomatic training at the Anwar Gargash Diplomatic Academy, earning a PgD in Diplomacy and International Relations in 2021.

Al Bastaki has been writing poetry since she was seven years old and has written hundreds of poems. She completed her first full volume of poetry 'House to House بيت لبيت' in 2018, based on oral histories from the Dubai Creek communities. In 2019, she won the ADMAF Creativity Award for this poetry collection, an excerpt of which was published in the Asymptote Journal and taught in universities in Japan, Taiwan, and the US. She is also an ambassador for Louvre Abu Dhabi and co-founded several literary networks, including JARA Collective and Untitled Chapters. Her thesis 'Al-Majaz: A Crossing' won the Best Thesis Award at the Harvard Center for Middle Eastern Studies.

figures.significance: Emirati actress of Achomi ancestry who appeared in Kuwaiti television and highlighted Achomi cultural identity through her performances.
figures.biography:
Huda Al-Khatib is an Emirati actress of Achomi ancestry who has appeared in television productions across the Gulf region. She notably appeared in the Kuwaiti TV comedy drama serial 'Al-Da'la' (الدعلة) where she spoke broken Arabic mixed with Persian/Achomi, bringing attention to the cultural identity of Achomi migrants and their linguistic heritage.

In her role, she was shown bringing her giddu (گِدو; old traditional Iranian hookah) and raging out in Achomi and Persian. The show featured a full scene in the 24th Episode where her character was shown learning the Arabic language and sang the Laristani/Achomi/Bastaki song 'Ghalyon ma teshn, ghori ma chai, yar nazanin, jaye to khali' by Yousif Hadi Bastaki. Her performances highlighted the challenges faced by Achomi migrants in adapting to Arabic-speaking environments while preserving their linguistic and musical heritage.
achievements: Appeared in Kuwaiti TV series Al-Da'la

figures.biography:
Fady Jameel serves as President of Community Jameel, the Abdul Latif Jameel family's international philanthropic organization. Along with Hassan Jameel, he has been a key figure in launching major academic and research collaborations that bear the family name. In October 2019, he participated in the signing ceremony that established the Abdul Latif Jameel Institute for Disease and Emergency Analytics at Imperial College London, alongside Imperial's president Alice Gast and Hassan Jameel.

Fady was also present at the September 2018 founding of the MIT Abdul Latif Jameel Clinic for Machine Learning in Health, participating in the signing ceremony at MIT with MIT President L. Rafael Reif and Hassan Jameel. His leadership in Community Jameel has been crucial in establishing the organization as a major force in international philanthropy, supporting initiatives in health, education, poverty alleviation, and scientific research across multiple continents.

figures.biography: Mohammed Jameel of Saudi Arabia's Abdul Latif Jameel Corporation played a crucial role in the development of one of the world's most influential development economics research centers. In 2005, he provided the endowment that allowed MIT's economics department to rename and expand the 'Poverty Action Lab' to become the 'Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL).'
achievements: Endowed J-PAL at MIT in 2005
Supported major academic research in development economics

figures.name: Walid Ahmed Juffali
figures.title: Chairman
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of E. A. Juffali and Brothers and multiple major Saudi companies
figures.significance: Assumed leadership of Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise after his father's death and became a prominent business figure with interests spanning multiple industries.
figures.biography:
Walid Ahmed Juffali was born in Jeddah on 30 April 1955, the son of Ahmed Abdullah Juffali, founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. His mother was Suad bint Ibrahim Al Husseini, granddaughter of Kamil of Jerusalem and niece of Hajj Amin of Jerusalem. After completing elementary and secondary education between Jeddah and Switzerland, he received a bachelor's degree in International Business and Political Sciences from the University of San Diego in 1977. He later earned a doctorate in neurosciences from Imperial College in 2012, with his PhD thesis entitled 'A Novel Algorithm for Detection and Prediction of Neural Anomalies.'

Walid assumed the chairmanship of E. A. Juffali and Brothers after his father's death in 1994. He also served as chairman of Saudi American Bank, The Saudi Cement Company, and sat on boards of Saudi Hollandi Bank and many other companies. In 2005, he was appointed chair of Dow Chemical Arabia and Siemens Arabia. He owned his personal investment company 'W Investments' and had significant stakes in major international companies, including 5% of Hitachi and 11% of Manchester United. Walid developed a great interest in brain theory and launched the annual brain forum conference, which by 2016 hosted over 1,400 participants from 31 countries.

Walid's personal life included high-profile divorces, including a £75 million settlement with Christina Estrada in 2016, which was the largest such settlement in English legal history at the time. He died in Zürich, Switzerland on 20 July 2016 after a lengthy battle with cancer. His estate was inherited by his brother Sheikh Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali, who also became the legal guardian of his five daughters.
achievements: Chairman of E. A. Juffali and Brothers, Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise
Chairman of Saudi American Bank and The Saudi Cement Company
Earned doctorate in neurosciences from Imperial College
Founded annual brain forum conference with international participation
Had significant stakes in Hitachi (5%) and Manchester United (11%)
Donated at least $3 million to the Clinton Presidential Center

figures.name: Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali
figures.title: Sheikh and Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Inheritor of the Juffali business empire and legal guardian
figures.significance: Became the inheritor of Walid Juffali's estate and legal guardian of his five daughters after Walid's death in 2016.
figures.biography:
Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali is one of the sons of Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Juffali, founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. He is the full brother of Walid bin Ahmed Al Juffali and Tarek bin Ahmed Al Juffali. After his brother Walid's death in July 2016, Khalid became the inheritor of Walid's estate and took on the responsibility of being the legal guardian of Walid's five daughters.

As part of the Al-Juffali family, Khalid belongs to the wealthy and secretive family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim, who are members of the Bani Khalid dynasty. The family built one of Saudi Arabia's most successful business empires through E. A. Juffali and Brothers, which became the largest privately owned enterprise in the country.

figures.name: Tarek bin Ahmed Al Juffali
figures.title: Business Executive
figures.roleDescription: Member of the Al Juffali business family
figures.significance: One of the sons of Ahmed Al Juffali and brother to Walid and Khalid, part of the family leadership of Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise.
figures.biography:
Tarek bin Ahmed Al Juffali is one of the sons of Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Juffali, the founder of E. A. Juffali and Brothers. He is the full brother of Walid bin Ahmed Al Juffali and Khalid bin Ahmed Al Juffali. As part of the Al-Juffali family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim, Tarek belongs to the wealthy business dynasty that are members of the Bani Khalid.

The Al-Juffali family built their business empire through E. A. Juffali and Brothers, which became Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise. The family has been instrumental in introducing various international companies and industries to Saudi Arabia, including power generation, telecommunications, and vehicle manufacturing.

figures.name: Reema Juffali
figures.title: Racing Driver
figures.roleDescription: Saudi Arabian racing driver and motorsport pioneer
figures.significance: Notable Saudi Arabian racing driver who competed as a wildcard entry in the 2024 F1 Academy season at Jeddah.
figures.biography:
Reema Juffali is a Saudi Arabian racing driver born in 1992. She made history as one of the pioneering female racing drivers from Saudi Arabia, competing in GT racing before moving into single-seater competition. In 2024, she participated in the F1 Academy season as a wildcard entry for the opening round at the Jeddah Street Circuit.

During her F1 Academy debut weekend in Jeddah, Reema faced challenges typical of motorsport competition. In the first race, she was involved in a collision with Amna Al Qubaisi on lap 2, and in the second race, she crashed out on lap 6, which caused the sole safety car period of that race. Despite the difficulties, her participation represented an important milestone for women in motorsport in Saudi Arabia and the broader Middle East region.
achievements: Pioneering female racing driver from Saudi Arabia
Competed in GT racing
Wildcard entry in 2024 F1 Academy season at Jeddah

figures.name: Abdul Rahman Al-Amoudi
figures.title: Former American Muslim Activist
figures.roleDescription: Founder of American Muslim Council, convicted on terrorism-related charges
figures.significance: Founded the American Muslim Council and was involved in a plot to assassinate Saudi Crown Prince Abdullah.
figures.biography:
Abdul Rahman Al-Amoudi was born in the Province of Eritrea in Ethiopia and moved to the United States in 1979, becoming a U.S. citizen in 1996. Before his arrest, he was one of America's best-known Muslim activists who met with senior Clinton and Bush administration officials and founded the American Muslim Foundation. He played a role in establishing a U.S. Department of Defense Muslim chaplain program and was invited by the Bush White House to speak at a Washington National Cathedral prayer service after 9/11.

In 2003, Al-Amoudi was indicted for illegal dealings with Libya and involvement in a plot financed by Muammar Gaddafi to assassinate Saudi Crown Prince Abdullah. He pleaded guilty in 2004 to three federal criminal charges including violating terrorism-related sanctions and was sentenced to 23 years in prison. He was automatically stripped of his U.S. citizenship following his conviction.
achievements: Founded American Muslim Council
Established U.S. Department of Defense Muslim chaplain program

figures.name: Muhammad Sa'eed Al Amoudi
figures.title: Saudi Arabian Journalist and Literary Critic
figures.roleDescription: Prominent 20th-century Saudi journalist, editor, and member of Consultative Assembly
figures.significance: A pioneering writer in 20th-century Hejaz, known for classical Arabic text editing and literary criticism.
figures.biography:
Muhammad bin Sa'eed Al-Amoudi was born in Mecca in 1905 to a merchant family descended from the Hejazi saint Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Bakri. After graduating from Al-Falah School, he initially worked in commerce with his father before transitioning to government positions. He headed the editorial board of the General Post and Telegraph Authority and served as editor-in-chief of its magazine until 1971.

From 1951 to 1954, he was a member of the Saudi Consultative Assembly and later served as editor-in-chief of the Muslim World League magazine from 1965 to 1978. He was chosen by the Ministry of Education for various committees and was one of the founding members of the Publishing and Writing Committee. Al-Amoudi was recognized as an inspiring poet and distinguished writer, publishing works on history, poetry, and short stories. He died in Mecca on February 16, 1991, after a long illness at age 86.
achievements: First prize from Al-Hilal magazine for best poem (1933)
Member of Saudi Consultative Assembly (1951-1954)
Published complete works in 3 volumes (2007)

figures.name: Omar Abdulrahman Ahmed Al Raaki Al Amoudi
figures.nameAr: عُمَر عَبْد الرَّحْمٰن أَحْمَد الرَّاقِي الْعَمُودِيّ
figures.title: Former Professional Footballer
figures.roleDescription: Emirati attacking midfielder and winger, formerly of Al Ain and UAE national team
figures.significance: Ranked first among top Asian players in 2012 and considered one of the most promising football talents from the region.
figures.biography:
Omar Abdulrahman was born in Riyadh in 1991 to a working-class family from the Al-Amoudi lineage, descending from 13th century Sufi saint Sa'eed ibn Isa Al-Amoudi. After being spotted by a scout at Al Malaz yard, he joined Al Ain's youth academy in 2008 when his family was granted UAE citizenship. He made his professional debut in 2009 and became a first-team regular despite suffering multiple cruciate ligament injuries.

Throughout his career, Abdulrahman won multiple UAE Pro-League titles with Al Ain and was voted Emirati Player of the Year multiple times. He had a two-week trial with Manchester City in 2012 and later played for Al Hilal, Al Jazira, and Shabab Al-Ahli. Internationally, he represented the UAE in major tournaments including the 2012 Olympics, 2015 AFC Asian Cup, and won the 2013 Arabian Gulf Cup where he was named Player of the Tournament. ESPN FC ranked him first among the top ten Asian players of 2012.
achievements: UAE Pro-League champion multiple times
Arabian Gulf Cup winner and MVP (2013)
AFC Asian Cup third place (2015)
Emirati Player of the Year multiple times
ESPN FC Top Asian Player (2012)

figures.name: Samia al-Amoudi
figures.title: Obstetrician, Gynecologist and Healthcare Activist
figures.roleDescription: Head of Sheikh Mohammed Hussein Al-Amoudi Center of Excellence in breast cancer
figures.significance: First woman from the Gulf Cooperation Council to sit on the Union for International Cancer Control board and pioneering breast cancer awareness advocate.
figures.biography:
Samia al-Amoudi graduated from King Abdulaziz University's medical college in 1981, among the first doctors from that institution. In April 2006, she diagnosed herself with breast cancer and underwent chemotherapy, completing treatment in 2012. She became the first Saudi woman to write publicly about her experience with the disease, publishing a series of articles in Al Madinah newspaper and appearing on daily TV shows to raise awareness.

Since 2012, she has served as head of the Sheikh Mohammed Hussein Al-Amoudi Center of Excellence in breast cancer and scientific chair for breast cancer at King Abdulaziz University. She has been recognized internationally for her advocacy work, receiving the International Women of Courage Award from U.S. Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice in 2007. Arabian Business ranked her among the most powerful Arab women, placing her 5th on their Power 100 list in 2010.
achievements: International Women of Courage Award (2007)
Ranked 5th on Arabian Business Power 100 list (2010)
First Gulf woman on Union for International Cancer Control board
Published 'Breast Cancer, Break the Silence' (2007)

figures.name: Salem Bin Mahfouz

figures.roleDescription: Saudi entrepreneur and banking pioneer
figures.significance: Founded the first private Saudi bank and became personal banker to the Saudi royal family.
figures.biography: Salem Ahmed bin Mahfouz was a Saudi entrepreneur who rose from being an illiterate small-time money-changer to becoming the founder of the National Commercial Bank (NCB), the first private bank in Saudi Arabia. He established himself as a significant figure in Saudi banking and finance, eventually becoming the personal banker of the Saudi royal family. His success in banking laid the foundation for one of Saudi Arabia's most prominent business families. Salem handed management of NCB, which became the largest bank in the country, to his son Khalid sometime in the 1980s, establishing a banking dynasty that would continue to influence Saudi finance for decades.
achievements: Founded the National Commercial Bank, the first private Saudi bank

figures.name: Mahfouz Marei Mubarak bin Mahfouz
figures.title: Saudi Arabian Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Businessman and philanthropist with ties to British institutions
figures.significance: Saudi businessman involved in philanthropy and British institutions, later subject to cash-for-honors allegations.
figures.biography:
Mahfouz Marei Mubarak bin Mahfouz was born on December 14, 1969, in Mecca, Saudi Arabia. He became a prominent businessman with extensive connections to British institutions and charitable organizations. He was made a Foundation Fellow at Pembroke College, Oxford in 2012 and became the inaugural Bredon Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge in July 2013. The Mahfouz Building at Pembroke College, Oxford was named after him, as was a room at Wolfson College, Cambridge. He was admitted to the Freedom of the City of London in December 2013 and served as a Vice-President of the British Forces Foundation.

Mahfouz became involved with Prince Charles's charitable organizations, serving as an Ambassador and Community Patron for The Prince's Foundation. He donated over £1.5 million to royal charities and met with Prince Harry in 2013 and 2014, contributing £50,000 to Harry's charity Sentebale and £10,000 to Walking With The Wounded. However, Harry stated in 2021 that he severed ties with Mahfouz in 2015 after expressing 'growing concerns' about his motives. Mahfouz was awarded an honorary Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in 2016 'for services to charities in the UK,' though this later became subject to cash-for-honors allegations involving claims that the honor was obtained through donations rather than merit.
achievements: Made Foundation Fellow at Pembroke College, Oxford (2012)
Became inaugural Bredon Fellow of Wolfson College, Cambridge (2013)
Awarded honorary Commander of the Order of the British Empire (2016)
Admitted to the Freedom of the City of London (2013)

figures.name: Khalid Ali Bugshan
figures.title: Saudi Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman involved in art transactions and legal proceedings
figures.significance: Saudi businessman who arranged art sales and was questioned in connection with French political corruption investigations.
figures.biography:
Khalid Ali Bugshan is a Saudi businessman who became involved in high-profile French political investigations in the 2010s. He was connected to the sale of two Andries van Eertvelt paintings that were allegedly sold to a Malaysian attorney, with the transaction being arranged by Bugshan according to Claude Guéant's claims.

In March 2015, Bugshan was taken into police custody for questioning by French authorities in connection with the investigation into alleged Libyan financing of Nicolas Sarkozy's 2007 presidential campaign. While he was not charged, his arrest came on the same day as Claude Guéant, Sarkozy's former chief of staff, who claimed that money he received was payment for the art sale that Bugshan had arranged. Bugshan's involvement highlighted the complex web of international business relationships that became scrutinized during the French political corruption investigations.

figures.name: Abdullah Ahmed Bugshan
figures.title: Chairman of Hadhramaut Culture Foundation
figures.roleDescription: Saudi businessman and cultural patron funding Yemeni musical heritage projects
figures.significance: Saudi businessman who serves as Chairman of the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation and is the main sponsor of the Heritage Symphonies cultural project promoting Yemeni folk music.
figures.biography:
Abdullah Ahmed Bugshan is a prominent Saudi businessman who serves as Chairman of the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation. He is the principal financial backer of the Heritage Symphonies project, a major cultural initiative founded in 2019 that aims to promote and disseminate Yemeni folk music by integrating it into orchestral performances that combine traditional instruments with Western orchestral elements.

Bugshan's foundation has provided substantial funding for multiple international concerts, including performances in Malaysia, Egypt, France, Qatar, Kuwait, and other countries. The project has achieved significant commercial success, with concerts regularly selling out across the Arab world. However, his involvement has also drawn criticism from some Yemeni music critics who have questioned whether such generous funding might have been more effectively invested in reviving local musical institutions in Yemen, and who have suggested that his financial influence has affected the project's musical execution and vision.
achievements: Founded and chairs the Hadhramaut Culture Foundation
Principal sponsor of the internationally successful Heritage Symphonies project
Funded major orchestral concerts promoting Yemeni culture across multiple countries

figures.name: Qasim bin Zainal Alireza
figures.title: Ottoman Parliamentarian
figures.roleDescription: Deputy for Jeddah in Ottoman parliament
figures.significance: First Alireza family member to hold political office, serving as deputy for Jeddah in the Ottoman parliament of 1908.
figures.biography:
Qasim bin Zainal Alireza was the son of the family patriarch Zainal Alireza and represented the family's entry into formal political representation. In 1908, he was elected as the deputy for Jeddah within the Ottoman parliament, marking an important milestone in the family's political involvement during the final years of Ottoman rule in the Hijaz region.

His election to the Ottoman parliament demonstrated the family's growing influence and prominence in Jeddah's commercial and political circles. This political engagement would set a precedent for future generations of the Alireza family, who continued to play significant roles in Saudi Arabian politics and governance after the establishment of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
achievements: Elected deputy for Jeddah in Ottoman parliament of 1908

figures.name: Muhammad Ali Zainal Alireza
figures.title: Educator and Pearl Merchant
figures.roleDescription: Educational pioneer and international pearl merchant
figures.significance: Established the first schools in Jeddah (1905) and Mecca (1912) for poor children, and became a successful pearl merchant in Paris and Bombay.
figures.biography:
Muhammad Ali Zainal Alireza, another son of Zainal Alireza, was a pioneering figure in education and international commerce. In 1905, he established the first school in Jeddah for poor children, offering free tuition at a time when education was not widely available. He followed this in 1912 by opening another school in Mecca, also providing free education to underprivileged children. As the region was still part of the Ottoman Empire, the language of instruction was Ottoman Turkish.

After establishing these educational institutions, Muhammad Ali expanded his business interests internationally, becoming a successful pearl merchant with operations in Paris and Bombay. He specialized in importing Persian Gulf pearls to both European and Indian markets, demonstrating the family's early international business acumen. Though his schools were eventually closed due to violations of British laws, his educational legacy marked an important early contribution to learning in the Hijaz region.
achievements: Established first school in Jeddah (1905)
Founded school in Mecca (1912)
Successful pearl merchant in Paris and Bombay

figures.name: Abdullah Alireza
figures.title: Founder of Rezayat Group
figures.roleDescription: Founded major Gulf business conglomerate
figures.significance: Founded the Rezayat Group in Kuwait in 1949, which became a major Gulf business conglomerate with over 16,000 employees.
figures.biography:
Abdullah Alireza was a member of the prominent House of Alireza merchant family and was educated in Bombay before beginning his career as secretary for Karl Twitchell, Director of the Saudi Arabian Mining Syndicate. Twitchell had previously been the local representative of California-Arabian Standard Oil Company (CASOC), one of the partners that created Saudi Aramco in 1933. Abdullah became liaison officer for CASOC, serving King AbdulAziz as an interpreter, most notably at the historic ceremony in Dhahran on 1 May 1939, when the King turned the wheel to load the first shipment of Saudi oil.

During World War II, Abdullah was asked to join the Allied administration in Iran with responsibility for distributing essential foodstuffs and supplies throughout the country. On a visit to Kuwait in 1949, he saw opportunities to develop business in the Kuwait-Saudi Arabia Neutral Zone and decided to base his new group in Kuwait. With the help of his sons Teymour and Fahd, he steadily expanded operations into other Gulf states and Saudi Arabia, building important relationships with major Western and Asian contractors that provided a foundation for growth across multiple continents.
achievements: Interpreter for King AbdulAziz at first Saudi oil shipment ceremony (1939)
Founded Rezayat Group in Kuwait (1949)
Built international business conglomerate with 16,000+ employees

figures.name: Ahmed Alireza
figures.title: Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Third-generation family business leader
figures.significance: Zainal Alireza's grandson who led the family business interests until his death in 2012.
figures.biography:
Ahmed Alireza was the grandson of family patriarch Zainal Alireza and represented the third generation of family leadership in the House of Alireza business empire. He played a significant role in continuing the family's commercial legacy and overseeing the expansion of their business interests across various sectors.

Ahmed died in 2012, after which the family business leadership passed to his surviving children. His sons Khaled, Mohammed, Abdullah, Hisham, Yousef, and his daughter Mariam, along with some of their children including Aamer, Hatim, and Sulaiman, continue to serve as executives for the House of Alireza's commercial interests, including major companies like SISCO, Tusdeer, and Xenel.
achievements: Led third generation of House of Alireza business empire

figures.name: Khalid Alireza
figures.title: CEO of Xenel Group
figures.roleDescription: Current CEO of major family business conglomerate
figures.significance: Current CEO of Xenel Group and son of Ahmed Alireza, leading the fourth generation of family business operations.
figures.biography:
Khalid Alireza is the son of Ahmed Alireza and currently serves as CEO of the Xenel Group, a diversified company based in Jeddah that was founded in 1973 by descendants of the House of Alireza. Xenel, whose name is a spelling variation honoring family patriarch Zainal Alireza, has grown into a major conglomerate with expertise in energy, petrochemicals, construction, infrastructure development, healthcare, industrial services, information technologies, logistics, real estate, and global investing.

As part of the fourth generation of the Alireza family business leadership, Khalid works alongside his siblings Mohammed, Abdullah, Hisham, Yousef, and sister Mariam, as well as other family members including Aamer, Hatim, and Sulaiman, who serve as executives across the House of Alireza's various commercial interests including SISCO, Tusdeer, and Xenel.
achievements: CEO of Xenel Group
Fourth-generation family business leader

figures.name: Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi
figures.title: Princess and Mother
figures.roleDescription: Wife of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma
figures.significance: Mother of several prominent Iranian princes and princesses in the Qajar dynasty.
figures.biography:
Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi (1900-1984) was one of the eight wives of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma, a prominent member of the Qajar dynasty and former Prime Minister of Iran. She bore him seven children between 1918 and 1927, including Princess Mahsama, Prince Jamshid, Prince Kaveh, Prince Ali Naghi, Prince Alidad, Princess Bilqis (who died young in 1927), and Prince Hafez.

As part of the extended Farman Farma family, she lived through the major political transitions of 20th century Iran, including the fall of the Qajar dynasty and the rise of the Pahlavi dynasty. Her husband was known for ensuring all his children, including daughters, received both classical Iranian training and modern Western education. Fatemeh Khanum lived for nearly eight decades, witnessing the transformation of Iran from a traditional monarchy to a modern state.

figures.name: Ali Naghi Farman Farmaian
figures.title: Prince
figures.roleDescription: Son of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma
figures.significance: Member of the prominent Qajar dynasty Farman Farma family.
figures.biography:
Prince Ali Naghi Farman Farmaian was born in 1923 as the son of Prince Abdol-Hossein Farman Farma and Fatemeh Khanum Alinaghi (Shirazi). He was part of the large Farman Farma family, one of the most influential political dynasties in Qajar and early Pahlavi Iran. His father served as the 11th Prime Minister of Iran and held numerous other high governmental positions.

Like his siblings, Ali Naghi was raised with both traditional Iranian values and modern Western education, as his father believed in preparing his children for the changing political landscape of Iran. He lived through the major transformations of 20th century Iran, including the transition from Qajar to Pahlavi rule and later the Islamic Revolution of 1979.

figures.name: Zahid Maleque
figures.title: Politician and Former Health Minister
figures.roleDescription: Bangladesh Awami League politician and former minister of health and family welfare
figures.significance: Former Bangladesh health minister who received WHO award for reducing child mortality and handled COVID-19 pandemic response.
figures.biography: Zahid Maleque was born on April 11, 1959, in Garpara Union, Manikganj Sadar Upazila, Manikgonj District in the then East Pakistan. He completed his undergraduate and postgraduate degrees in English from the University of Dhaka. His father was Colonel M. A. Maleque, a former minister, former mayor of Dhaka, and member of parliament. Maleque built a successful business career, serving as chairman of multiple companies including Sun Life Insurance Company Limited, Bangladesh Thai Aluminium Limited, and several other enterprises. He entered politics representing the Manikganj-3 constituency, first contesting in 2001 but losing to the Bangladesh Nationalist Party candidate. He won the seat in 2008 and was subsequently re-elected in 2014 and 2018. As health minister, Maleque received a WHO award in 2016 on behalf of the health ministry for reducing child mortality. During the COVID-19 pandemic, he faced significant criticism for his handling of the crisis, including issues with protective equipment quality and testing kit availability, with several parliamentary colleagues calling for his removal in June 2020.
achievements: WHO award recipient for reducing child mortality (2016)
Three-term Member of Parliament for Manikganj-3 (2009-2024)

figures.name: Mustafa Zahid
figures.title: Musician and Singer
figures.roleDescription: Pakistani music composer, singer, and lead vocalist of rock band Roxen
figures.significance: Gained international success with Bollywood soundtracks and is among the top three most-followed Pakistani artists on Spotify.
figures.biography: Mustafa Zahid was born on December 18, 1984, in Lahore, Pakistan. He comes from a notable musical family, being the nephew of Ali Azmat, the former lead vocalist of the famous rock band Junoon, and is also related to the late Sufi kalam singer, actor, director, and producer Inayat Hussain Bhatti. As a child, he never initially planned to become a singer, but his path changed when he was asked to perform at a college event where he sang 'Bheegi Bheegi Raaton Mein,' which motivated him to pursue music professionally. Mustafa formed the rock band Roxen in 2004 and serves as both the band leader and lead vocalist. His breakthrough came in mid-2007 when two of his songs were featured in the Bollywood movie Awarapan, including the hits 'Toh Phir Aao' and 'Tera Mera Rishta.' Since then, he has sung over a dozen Bollywood songs, including major hits in films such as Aashiqui 2 and Ek Villain. Roxen's debut album, Roxen-E-Dewaar, was released in late 2006 and topped charts across South Asia for nearly six weeks. Universal Music signed Mustafa Zahid and Roxen for worldwide album releases, and he has achieved remarkable digital success, becoming one of the top three most-followed Pakistani artists on Spotify with over 5.5 million followers.
achievements: Lead vocalist and band leader of successful rock band Roxen
Singer of major Bollywood hit songs in films like Aashiqui 2 and Ek Villain
Over 5.5 million followers on Spotify, among top three Pakistani artists
Debut album Roxen-E-Dewaar topped South Asian charts for six weeks
Signed with Universal Music for worldwide releases

figures.name: Salma Zahid
figures.title: Canadian Politician
figures.roleDescription: Member of Parliament representing Scarborough Centre in the House of Commons of Canada
figures.significance: First MP to wear a hijab in the Canadian House of Commons and advocate for racialized women's rights.
figures.biography: Salma Zahid was born on May 26, 1970, in Coventry, England, and is a Pakistani Canadian politician. She holds a master's degree in educational management and administration from the University of London's Institute of Education, and an MBA from Quaid-i-Azam University in Pakistan. A mother of two, she has resided in Scarborough since 2000 and is a recipient of the Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal. Before entering politics, Zahid worked as a community organizer and held multiple positions with the Government of Ontario. She was elected as a Member of Parliament for Scarborough Centre in the 2015 Canadian federal election, succeeding Conservative Roxanne James. During the 42nd Parliament, she served as Vice-Chair for the Standing Committee on the Status of Women, where she was a spokesperson for racialized women. In February 2018, Zahid announced she was taking medical leave to treat Stage 4 Non-Hodgkin lymphoma. During her recovery from chemotherapy, she grew closer to her Islamic faith and began wearing a hijab for personal reasons. In May 2018, she made history by becoming the first MP to wear a hijab in the House of Commons. After completing six rounds of chemotherapy, she was declared cancer-free in July 2018 and returned to her parliamentary duties. She was re-elected in the 2019 Canadian federal election but has faced criticism for her positions on international issues, including attending events with Holocaust denialism publishers and calling for support of South Africa's genocide case against Israel in January 2024.
achievements: First Member of Parliament to wear a hijab in the Canadian House of Commons
Vice-Chair of the Standing Committee on the Status of Women
Recipient of the Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal
Successfully overcame Stage 4 Non-Hodgkin lymphoma
Re-elected Member of Parliament for Scarborough Centre

figures.name: Zahid Nisar Quraishi
figures.title: Federal Judge
figures.roleDescription: United States District Judge and first Muslim Article III federal judge
figures.significance: First Muslim Article III federal judge confirmed by the United States Senate and distinguished military veteran.
figures.biography: Zahid Nisar Quraishi was born in 1975 in New York City and raised in Fanwood, New Jersey, by his parents Shahida P. Quraishi and Dr. Nisar A. Quraishi, immigrants from Pakistan. He graduated from Scotch Plains-Fanwood High School in 1993, received his Bachelor of Arts from John Jay College of Criminal Justice in 1997, and his Juris Doctor from Rutgers Law School in 2000. After law school, he clerked for Judge Edwin Stern and worked as a litigation associate before joining the U.S. Army Judge Advocate General's Corps in 2003. He served two combat deployments in Iraq (2004 and 2006-2007) in Tikrit and Ramadi, providing legal counsel on detainee operations and military justice under intense combat conditions. He was honorably discharged as a Captain, receiving numerous decorations including the Bronze Star and Combat Action Badge. After military service, Quraishi worked as assistant chief counsel at ICE, Assistant U.S. Attorney for New Jersey (2008-2013), and later became a partner at Riker, Danzig law firm. He was appointed as a U.S. Magistrate Judge in 2019, becoming the first Asian-American on the federal bench in New Jersey. On March 30, 2021, President Biden nominated him to be a U.S. District Judge, and he was confirmed by the Senate on June 10, 2021, by an 81-16 vote, making history as the first Muslim-American Article III federal judge. As a judge, he has made notable rulings including blocking New Jersey gun industry lawsuit laws and ending the controversial county line ballot system in New Jersey primaries.
achievements: First Muslim-American to serve as Article III federal judge
First Asian-American federal judge in New Jersey
Bronze Star and Combat Action Badge recipient for Iraq War service
U.S. Army Judge Advocate General's Corps veteran with two combat deployments
Landmark ruling ending New Jersey's county line ballot system
Confirmed by Senate vote of 81-16 in 2021

figures.name: Ahmad Zahid Hamidi
figures.title: Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia
figures.roleDescription: Malaysian politician, UMNO President, and Deputy Prime Minister
figures.significance: Current Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia and UMNO President, first UMNO president after independence to not become prime minister.
figures.biography: Ahmad Zahid bin Hamidi was born on January 4, 1953, in Bagan Datuk, Perak, the eldest of nine children to Indonesian-born Malaysian parents. He was notably raised by a Chinese foster-father, Chen Jin Ting, and sold ice cream together for six years during elementary school, an experience he later cited when defending against accusations of being anti-Chinese. He studied at Sekolah Izzuddin Shah and graduated from University of Malaya in 1976, later earning a PhD in Communication from University of Putra Malaysia in 2008. Before politics, he worked as a banker for OCBC and held various corporate positions including directorships at Bank Simpanan Nasional and Permodalan Nasional Berhad. He began his political career in 1986 as political secretary to Najib Razak, was elected UMNO Youth Chief in 1996, and won the Bagan Datok parliamentary seat in 1995, which he has held continuously since. Zahid served in various ministerial positions under Abdullah Ahmad Badawi and Najib Razak from 2008-2018, including Minister of Defence (2009-2013), Minister of Home Affairs (2013-2015), and Deputy Prime Minister (2015-2018). After UMNO's defeat in 2018, he became party president and Leader of the Opposition. Despite leading BN to its worst electoral result in 2022, winning only 30 seats, he successfully negotiated a coalition government with Pakatan Harapan and was reappointed Deputy Prime Minister in December 2022, making him the first person to hold this position twice under different administrations. Throughout his career, Zahid has faced numerous corruption charges, including 87 charges involving RM114 million, though he was acquitted of 40 bribery charges in 2022 and received dismissals on remaining cases in 2023.
achievements: Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia (2015-2018, 2022-present)
President of UMNO since 2018
Minister of Defence and Home Affairs
Longest-serving MP for Bagan Datok constituency (1995-present)
Leader of Barisan Nasional coalition
Recipient of numerous Malaysian state honors including Dato' Seri titles

figures.title: Abstract Painter
figures.roleDescription: Saudi Arabian abstract painter
figures.significance: Notable Saudi Arabian artist specializing in abstract painting.
figures.biography:
Shalimar Sharbatly was born in 1971 and is recognized as a prominent figure in contemporary Saudi Arabian art. She is listed among the notable Saudi Arabian artists and is specifically known for her work in abstract painting. Her inclusion in the comprehensive list of Saudi Arabian artists demonstrates her significance in the kingdom's artistic landscape.

As part of the contemporary generation of Saudi artists, Sharbatly represents the growing recognition of Saudi Arabian visual arts on both national and international levels. Her specialization in abstract painting places her within the modern artistic movements that have emerged in Saudi Arabia as the country has undergone cultural and social transformations.

figures.name: Hussein Kamel al-Majid
figures.title: Minister of Military Industries
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi government official responsible for weapons programs
figures.significance: He was responsible for Iraq's Chemical, Biological and Nuclear Weapons program during the 1980s and provided crucial intelligence to Western agencies after his defection.
figures.biography:
Hussein Kamel al-Majid was born in 1954 and served as Iraq's Minister of Military Industries under Saddam Hussein's regime. He was married to Raghad Hussein, Saddam's eldest daughter, in 1983, making him both a government official and a member of Saddam's inner family circle. During the 1980s, he was responsible for Iraq's Chemical, Biological and Nuclear Weapons program, giving him extensive knowledge of the country's weapons of mass destruction capabilities.

In 1995, Hussein Kamel made the significant decision to defect from Iraq along with his brother Saddam Kamel and their wives. They fled to Jordan where King Hussein granted them asylum. During his time in exile, Hussein Kamel shared valuable information about Iraq's weapons programs with UNSCOM, the CIA, and MI6. However, his plans to oust Saddam with Western support were undermined when Saddam decided to reveal weapons information himself. In 1996, believing promises of pardons and facing increasing isolation in Jordan, the brothers returned to Iraq where they were killed in February 1996, allegedly by fellow clan members who declared them traitors.
achievements: Minister of Military Industries
Responsible for Iraq's WMD programs in the 1980s
Provided intelligence on Iraqi weapons to Western agencies

figures.name: Stanley Kamel
figures.title: American Actor
figures.roleDescription: Television and film actor best known for role as Dr. Charles Kroger
figures.significance: He was an American actor best known for playing Dr. Charles Kroger, the psychiatrist to the main character in the popular USA Network television series Monk.
figures.biography:
Stanley Kamel was born on January 1, 1943, to a Jewish family and was raised in South River, New Jersey. He attended Rutgers Preparatory School and Boston University, graduating with a BFA in 1965. Kamel started his acting career off-Broadway before transitioning to television in 1968. Over the decades, he appeared in numerous episodic television shows including The Mod Squad, Barney Miller, Kojak, Lou Grant, Hill Street Blues, L.A. Law, Murder, She Wrote, Star Trek: The Next Generation, and many others.

Kamel had recurring roles on several major television series, including Melrose Place in 1994 as Bruce Teller, the CEO of D&D Advertising, and Beverly Hills, 90210 as organized crime figure Anthony Marchette. However, he was best known for his role as Dr. Charles Kroger in the USA Network television series Monk, playing the patient and supportive psychiatrist to Adrian Monk (Tony Shalhoub) from 2002 until his death in 2008. Kamel never married and had no children. On April 8, 2008, he was found dead of a heart attack in his Hollywood Hills home at age 65. The seventh season premiere of Monk was dedicated to his memory.
achievements: Starred as Dr. Charles Kroger in the TV series Monk
Appeared in numerous popular television series over several decades

figures.name: Hussein Kamel
figures.title: Sultan of Egypt
figures.roleDescription: Ruler of Egypt during the British protectorate
figures.significance: He was the first person to hold the title of Sultan of Egypt since 1517 and ruled during the critical period when Egypt became a British protectorate during World War I.
figures.biography:
Hussein Kamel was born on November 21, 1853, as the second son of Khedive Ismail Pasha, who ruled Egypt from 1863 to 1879. He was declared Sultan of Egypt on December 19, 1914, after the occupying British forces had deposed his nephew, Khedive Abbas Hilmi II, on November 5, 1914. This marked the end of de jure Ottoman sovereignty over Egypt, which had been largely nominal since Muhammad Ali's seizure of power in 1805. Though presented as the re-establishment of the pre-Ottoman Egyptian sultanate, the newly created Sultanate of Egypt was effectively a British protectorate.

During his reign, Hussein Kamel faced significant challenges, including two assassination attempts in 1915. The first occurred on April 9, 1915, when an assassin targeted his carriage in Cairo, and the second on July 9, 1915, when a bomb was thrown at his carriage as he traveled to Friday prayers. Hussein Kamel died on October 9, 1917. Upon his death, his only son, Prince Kamal el Dine Hussein, declined the succession, and Hussein Kamel's brother Ahmed Fuad ascended the throne as Fuad I.
achievements: First Sultan of Egypt since 1517
Founder of the Order of Muhammad Ali
Founder of the Order of Ismail
Founder of the Order of the Nile

figures.name: Kamel El Basha
figures.title: Palestinian Actor
figures.roleDescription: Actor, theatre director, teacher, and translator
figures.significance: He is a prominent Palestinian actor who won the prestigious Volpi Cup for Best Actor at the Venice International Film Festival for his performance in 'The Insult'.
figures.biography:
Kamel El Basha was born around 1962 in Malha (al-Maliha), then a thriving Palestinian village, now a suburb in south-western Jerusalem, Israel. He recalls being five years old and fleeing his home with his family during the 1967 war. As a young man, he was imprisoned by the Israeli authorities for two years. He studied theatre from 1979 to 1983 and later studied theatre in Baghdad, Iraq. After his release from prison, he began his career as a stage actor, making a notable debut in the Arabic version of Brecht's 'The Exception and the Rule'.

El Basha served as artistic director of the Palestinian National Theater in Jerusalem from 2007 to 2011, and as artistic director of Quds Art from 2012. He came to international attention for his role as Palestinian refugee Yasser Abdallah Salameh in the 2017 film 'The Insult' by Lebanese director Ziad Doueiri, for which he won the Volpi Cup for Best Actor at the 74th Venice International Film Festival. He has appeared in several other notable films including 'Love, Theft and Other Entanglements' (2015), 'The Reports on Sarah and Saleem' (2018), and the 2023 British heist series 'Culprits'. As of 2024, he lives in East Jerusalem with his Palestinian Christian wife.
achievements: Won Volpi Cup for Best Actor at Venice International Film Festival 2017
Artistic director of Palestinian National Theater (2007-2011)
Best Actor prize at Series Mania for 'House of Gods' (2024)

figures.biography: Daoud El-Issa was the nephew of Issa El-Issa and played a significant role in continuing the family's journalistic legacy. He took over the manager position of the Falastin newspaper after his uncle's death, ensuring the continuation of this important Palestinian publication. In 1956, he established Al-Bilād newspaper in Jerusalem, further expanding the family's media influence. His contributions to journalism were recognized when he became a member of the Jordan Press Association in 1976, cementing his role in Arab media circles.

figures.name: Yousef El-Issa
figures.title: Co-founder of Falastin and Publisher
figures.roleDescription: Palestinian journalist and newspaper publisher
figures.significance: Co-founded Falastin newspaper in 1909 and later established Alif Bā' newspaper in Damascus, expanding Palestinian media influence.
figures.biography: Yousef El-Issa was Issa El-Issa's cousin and served as co-founder of the influential Falastin newspaper in 1909. His partnership with Issa was crucial in establishing one of the most important Palestinian publications of the early 20th century. Later expanding his media activities beyond Palestine, Yousef established the Alif Bā' newspaper in Damascus, Syria, which operated from 1930 to 1958. This expansion helped spread Palestinian nationalist ideas and Arab intellectual discourse throughout the Levant region.
achievements: Co-founded Falastin newspaper in 1909

figures.biography: Georgette El-Issa was a member of the prominent El-Issa family and distinguished herself through her work as a Palestinian philanthropist and activist. While the family was primarily known for their contributions to journalism and media, Georgette carved out her own path in social activism and charitable work. Her efforts contributed to Palestinian civil society and social causes, representing the family's broader commitment to Palestinian nationalism and community development beyond their media enterprises.
achievements: Palestinian philanthropist and activist

figures.name: Mohsin Issa
figures.title: British Billionaire Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Co-founder of EG Group and former CEO of Asda
figures.significance: British billionaire businessman who co-founded Euro Garages (EG Group) and served as CEO of supermarket chain Asda.
figures.biography: Mohsin Issa was born in July 1971 in Blackburn, Lancashire, to Indian Gujarati Muslim parents Vali and Zubeda who had immigrated from Gujarat, India in the 1960s. Growing up in modest circumstances in a terraced house, he was educated at Witton Park Academy. After working briefly in the clothing industry, he co-founded the plastic-packaging firm Europlast in 1993 with his older brother Zakir. In 2001, Mohsin and his brother Zuber co-founded Euro Garages with the acquisition of a single petrol station in Bury, Greater Manchester, which they expanded into a major international business. In October 2020, as part of a consortium with TDR Capital, the brothers became majority stakeholders in British supermarket chain Asda, with Mohsin serving as CEO from August 2021 to September 2024. He was appointed Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in 2020 for services to business and charity.
achievements: Co-founded EG Group in 2001

figures.name: Zuber Issa
figures.title: British Billionaire Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Co-founder of EG Group and owner of EG On The Move
figures.significance: British billionaire businessman who co-founded Euro Garages (EG Group) with his brother and later focused on his separate EG On The Move business.
figures.biography: Zuber Issa was born in June 1972 in Blackburn, Lancashire, to Indian Gujarati Muslim parents who had immigrated from Gujarat, India. He was educated at Witton Park Academy alongside his brother Mohsin. At age 17-18 in 1988/90, Zuber purchased a news kiosk in Preston called Euro News, which he expanded into a newsagent chain in busy mall locations across North-West England. In 1998, he became a shareholder in Europlast, the plastic-packaging firm co-founded by his brothers. In 2001, he co-founded Euro Garages with Mohsin, starting with a single petrol station and building it into a major international business. Initially responsible for strategy and acquisitions while Mohsin handled day-to-day operations, Zuber later stepped back from EG Group to focus on his separate business, EG On The Move. He sold his 22.5% stake in Asda in 2024 to TDR Capital and was appointed CBE in 2020.
achievements: Co-founded EG Group in 2001

figures.title: Princess of Jordan
figures.roleDescription: Saudi-born member of Jordanian royal family through marriage
figures.significance: Notable member of the Abunayyan family who married into the Jordanian royal family as wife of Prince Hashim bin Hussein.
figures.biography:
Princess Fahdah Hashim was born Fahdah Mohammed Abunayyan in Saudi Arabia. She is the daughter of Sheikh Mohammad bin Ibrahim bun Suleiman Abuniyyan and her maternal grandfather is Sheikh Turki bin Khaled bin Ahmed Al Sudairi, who served as head of the Saudi Human Rights Commission. On 6 January 2006, she became engaged to Prince Hashim bin Hussein, the younger son of King Hussein of Jordan and Queen Noor.

On 15 April 2006, she married Prince Hashim bin Hussein in Jordan, becoming a member of the Jordanian royal family. The couple has five children together: three daughters and two sons born between 2007 and 2019. Their children are Princess Haalah bint Hashim (born 6 April 2007), Princess Rayet Al-Noor bint Hashim (born 4 July 2008), Princess Fatima Al-Alia bint Hashim (born 5 November 2011), Prince Hussein Haidara bin Hashim (born 15 June 2015), and Prince Mohammad Al-Hassan bin Hashim (born 21 October 2019). On 1 June 2023, she attended the Wedding of Hussein, Crown Prince of Jordan, and Rajwa Al Saif.
achievements: Married into Jordanian royal family
Mother of five royal children

Sheikh Mohammad bin Ibrahim bun Suleiman Abuniyyan is the father of Princess Fahdah Hashim (born Fahdah Mohammed Abunayyan). He represents the Abunayyan merchant family from Saudi Arabia. His daughter's marriage to Prince Hashim bin Hussein in 2006 created a connection between the Abunayyan family and the Jordanian royal family.

Through his daughter's marriage, Sheikh Mohammad became connected to the Hashemite royal family of Jordan. His daughter has given birth to five royal grandchildren, continuing the family lineage within the Jordanian monarchy.

figures.biography:
Muhammad Abdullah Rashid Abunayyan serves as Chairman of the Board of Directors of ACWA Power, a leading Saudi Arabian company in the renewable energy sector. He has been involved in high-level discussions regarding energy cooperation and renewable energy development in the region.

In 2023, he held meetings with Mikayil Jabbarov, Azerbaijan's Minister of Economy, to discuss attracting investors to Azerbaijan's energy sector, boosting the efficiency of renewable energy, and increasing the share of alternative energy sources. These discussions highlighted the important role of renewable energy in economic development strategies and emphasized cooperation with ACWA Power as one of the leading companies in the renewable energy sector.

figures.name: Ramez Jeraisy
figures.title: Mayor of Nazareth
figures.roleDescription: Mayor who collaborated on industrial development projects
figures.significance: Mayor of Nazareth who worked with Israeli industrialist Stef Wertheimer to establish an industrial park promoting Arab-Jewish coexistence.
figures.biography:
Ramez Jeraisy served as Mayor of Nazareth, the mixed Muslim-Christian Arab city in Israel. He collaborated with Israeli industrialist Stef Wertheimer on establishing an industrial park in Nazareth that opened in April 2013. The project was designed as a unique model to promote the advancement of Arab-Jewish Israeli export companies, with Jews and Arabs working side by side.

In 2009, Jeraisy met with Pope Benedict XVI alongside Wertheimer at the site of the future industrial park, where the Pope gave his blessing to the project. The industrial park was part of Wertheimer's broader vision of using industry to create jobs and foster coexistence in the region, following five principles: exports, education, coexistence, community and culture.
achievements: Established industrial park in Nazareth promoting Arab-Jewish coexistence

figures.name: Surachai Danwattananusorn (Surachai Saedan)
figures.title: Political Activist and Former Political Prisoner
figures.roleDescription: Thai political activist, communist party member, and anti-monarchist leader
figures.significance: Last member of the Communist Party of Thailand to be pardoned and founder of the Red Siam political movement.
figures.biography:
Born Surachai Saedan on December 24, 1942, in Thaphaya Tumbol, Pak Phanang District, Nakhon Si Thammarat Province, he was the son of Yokyuan and Somchao Saedan. He became well known after the Thammasat University massacre as a prominent member of the Communist Party of Thailand. He was sentenced to death for murder and participation in a train robbery but received a royal pardon in 1988, making him the last CPT member to be pardoned.

After his release, Surachai entered mainstream politics, serving as a committee member of the Thai Rak Thai Party and later founding the Red Siam political movement with Jakrapob Penkair. He was known for his anti-monarchist views, which resulted in multiple arrests under Thailand's lèse majesté laws. He was sentenced to 7 years and 6 months in prison in 2012 but received a royal pardon in October 2013.

Following the Thai military coup of 2014, Surachai went into exile in neighboring Laos. On December 10, 2018, he disappeared from Vientiane along with two aides, who were later found murdered. He is believed to have been killed as well, representing part of a broader pattern of forced disappearances of Thai activists in exile.
achievements: Last member of Communist Party of Thailand to receive royal pardon
Founded Red Siam political movement
Served as Thai Rak Thai Party committee member

figures.name: Mohammad Fahad Muflih al-Qahtani
figures.title: Human Rights Activist
figures.roleDescription: Economics professor and human rights activist
figures.significance: Co-founder and leader of the Saudi Civil and Political Rights Association who was imprisoned for his human rights activism.
figures.biography:
Mohammad Fahad Muflih al-Qahtani was born in 1965 and has a PhD from Indiana University Bloomington in the United States. As of June 2012, he was employed as an economics professor at the Institute of Diplomatic Affairs of the Saudi Arabian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Al-Qahtani co-founded the Saudi Civil and Political Rights Association (ACPRA) in October 2009 together with Mohammed Saleh al-Bejadi and nine others, and continued to be active in the organization as of June 2012. In November 2008, he was among 20 human rights activists who started a two-day hunger strike protesting the imprisonment without fair trials of 11 activists.

Al-Qahtani was charged in a Saudi court on June 18, 2012, on 11 charges related to his human rights activism, including setting up an unlicensed organization, breaking allegiance to the ruler, and accusing the judiciary of allowing torture. On March 9, 2013, he was sentenced to ten years in prison followed by ten years of travel ban. He was shifted into solitary confinement on December 17, 2018, and carried out several hunger strikes in 2020 and 2021 in protest against prison conditions. In 2018, he was awarded the Right Livelihood Award together with other jailed activists Abdullah al-Hamid and Waleed Abulkhair. Al-Qahtani was released from prison on January 7, 2025, but was given a 10-year travel ban preventing him from visiting his family in the US.
achievements: Co-founded Saudi Civil and Political Rights Association
Awarded Right Livelihood Award in 2018
PhD from Indiana University Bloomington

figures.name: Muhammad Abdullah al-Qahtani
figures.title: Self-proclaimed Mahdi
figures.roleDescription: Spiritual leader of the Grand Mosque seizure
figures.significance: Self-proclaimed Mahdi who was declared the redeemer by insurgents during the 1979 Grand Mosque seizure in Mecca.
figures.biography:
Muhammad Abdullah al-Qahtani was the brother-in-law of Juhayman al-Otaybi, who declared him to be the Mahdi, or redeemer, believed to arrive on earth several years before Judgment Day. His followers emphasized that Al-Qahtani's name and his father's name were identical to the Prophet Muhammad's name and that of his father, developing the saying 'His and his father's names were the same as Mohammed's and his father's, and he had come to Makkah from the north' to justify their belief. Al-Qahtani and al-Otaybi had met while imprisoned together for sedition, when al-Otaybi claimed to have had a vision sent by God telling him that Qahtani was the Mahdi.

Their declared goal was to institute a theocracy in preparation for the imminent apocalypse, differing from earlier Wahhabi purists in that they were millenarians who rejected the monarchy and condemned the Wahhabi ulama. The date of their attack on the Grand Mosque, November 20, 1979, was the last day of the year 1399 according to the Islamic calendar, tying in with the tradition of the mujaddid, a person who appears at the turn of every century to revive Islam. Al-Qahtani was killed during the Saudi forces' recapture of the Grand Mosque, which lasted more than two weeks and officially left 255 people dead.

figures.name: Ali al-Tamimi
