figures.name: Ahlam Aref Ahmad al-Tamimi
figures.title: Convicted Terrorist
figures.roleDescription: Jordanian national convicted for assisting in the Sbarro restaurant suicide bombing
figures.significance: Known for assisting in carrying out the Sbarro restaurant suicide bombing in Jerusalem in 2001, which caused 145 casualties including 16 fatalities.
figures.biography:
Ahlam Aref Ahmad al-Tamimi was born in 1980 in Zarqa, Jordan, and originates from Nabi Salih, a Palestinian village in the central West Bank. She was a journalism student at Birzeit University in the West Bank when she became involved in terrorist activities. At age 20, she helped plan and participated in the Sbarro restaurant suicide bombing on August 9, 2001, escorting suicide bomber Izz al-Din Shuheil al-Masri to the target location. She used disguise techniques and her journalism skills to deflect attention, wearing a dress to appear like a 'Jewish tourist' rather than an Arab.

After the bombing, which caused 145 casualties including 16 fatalities (half of them children), Tamimi reported on the attack in her part-time journalism job. She was convicted by an Israeli military tribunal and received 16 consecutive life sentences plus 15 years in prison. However, she was released in October 2011 as part of the Gilad Shalit prisoner exchange and exiled to Jordan. Since her release, she has hosted a Jordanian talk show called 'Nasim Al-Ahrar' (Breeze of the Free) on Hamas-affiliated Al-Quds TV, dealing with Palestinian prisoners in Israeli prisons. In 2013, the U.S. Justice Department filed criminal charges against her, and there is a $5 million reward for her capture.

figures.name: Ahed Tamimi
figures.title: Palestinian Activist
figures.roleDescription: Palestinian activist from the village of Nabi Salih
figures.significance: Known for confronting Israeli soldiers and became a symbol of Palestinian resistance, gaining international attention after a viral slapping incident in 2017.
figures.biography:
Ahed Tamimi was born on January 31, 2001, in Nabi Salih, a small village in the West Bank to activist Bassem Tamimi and his wife Nariman. Her father is a cousin of Ahlam Tamimi, known for assisting in the Sbarro restaurant bombing. From an early age, she has been involved in protests against Israeli settlements and detention of Palestinians. At age 11, she was commended by Palestinian president Mahmoud Abbas for attempting to intervene during her mother's arrest in August 2012, and gained international media attention when confronting an Israeli soldier who arrested her older brother.

On December 15, 2017, during a demonstration in Nabi Salih, Tamimi, along with her mother and cousin, was filmed slapping, kicking, and shoving Israeli soldiers outside their home. This occurred after her 15-year-old cousin Mohammed Tamimi was shot in the head with a rubber-coated steel bullet during the protest. The footage went viral, leading to her arrest on December 19. She agreed to a plea bargain and served eight months in prison, being released on July 29, 2018. In November 2023, she was arrested again on suspicion of inciting violence and held for almost three weeks before being released on November 29 as part of a hostage exchange agreement between Israel and Hamas. She has been described as one of the new symbols of Palestinian resistance and published a memoir titled 'They Called Me a Lioness' in 2022.
achievements: Published memoir 'They Called Me a Lioness'
Symbol of Palestinian resistance
International recognition as activist

figures.name: Al-Qa'qa' ibn Amr al-Tamimi
figures.nameAr: القعقاع بن عمرو بن مالك التميمي الراعي
figures.title: Arab Muslim Commander and General
figures.roleDescription: Commander and general in the Rashidun army from the tribe of Banu Tamim
figures.significance: Known as a successful military commander who took part in the victorious battles of Yarmouk against the Byzantine Empire and al-Qadisiyyah against the Sasanian Empire.
figures.biography:
Al-Qa'qa' ibn Amr al-Tamimi was an Arab Muslim commander and general in the Rashidun army who belonged to the tribe of Banu Tamim. He and his tribe converted to Islam possibly during the time of Ahnaf ibn Qais, though for a brief period he and other Tamim joined the force of the false prophetess Sajah bint al-Harith before she was subdued during the Ridda wars. He later carried on a successful military campaign under Khalid bin Walid, helping to suppress another false prophet, Tulayha, in the Battle of Buzakha.

Al-Qa'qa' took part in numerous important battles during the Muslim conquest of Iraq and Syria, including the Battle of Chains where he aided Khalid ibn al-Walid in defeating the Sassanid champion Hormuzan. He served under Khalid in the famous battle of Yarmouk against the Byzantine Empire as part of the mobile guard elite cavalry. The Caliph Abu Bakr praised him as equal to a thousand men, so Caliph Umar sent only Qa'qa' and a handful of bodyguards in the first wave of reinforcements to Al-Qadisiyyah. At the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah in November 636, he played a crucial role in the Muslim victory against the Sasanian Empire, using ingenious tactics such as disguising camels to look like monsters to frighten the Sassanid horses and leading successful charges that ultimately routed the Persian army.
achievements: Participated in Battle of Yarmouk
Led forces at Battle of al-Qadisiyyah
Praised by Caliph Abu Bakr as equal to a thousand men

figures.name: Saleh al-Fawzan
figures.title: Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Islamic scholar and fourth Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia
figures.significance: Senior Salafi scholar who became Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia in 2025.
figures.biography: Saleh al-Fawzan was born on 28 September 1935 in Ash-Shamasiyyah in the Al-Qassim Province of Saudi Arabia. He belongs to the Al-Wadain branch of the Al-Shammās clan of the Ad-Dawāsir tribe. His father died during his early childhood, and he was raised by his family. He learned the Quran from local mosque imam Ḥamūd ibn Sulaymān At-Tilāl. Al-Fawzan completed his education at Al-Faysaliyyah School in Buraydah in 1952 and was appointed as a primary school teacher. He then graduated from the Ma'had Al-'Ilmī in Buraydah in 1958 and from the College of Sharīʿah in Riyadh in 1962. He obtained a master's degree and doctorate in fiqh, focusing on inheritance law and Islamic food rulings respectively. After completing his doctorate, he taught at various Islamic institutions and served as head of Saudi Arabia's Supreme Court of Justice. In 2013, he was appointed as a member of the Council of Senior Scholars. On 22 October 2025, King Salman appointed al-Fawzan as Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia, succeeding Abdulaziz Al al-Sheikh.
achievements: Appointed Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia in 2025
Member of Council of Senior Scholars
Head of Saudi Arabia's Supreme Court of Justice
Director of Higher Institute of Judiciary

figures.name: Ali Abdullah Saleh
figures.title: President of Yemen
figures.roleDescription: First president of unified Yemen and former president of North Yemen
figures.significance: Long-serving Yemeni president who ruled for over three decades before being killed by Houthis in 2017.
figures.biography: Ali Abdullah Saleh was born on 21 March 1947 to a poor Zaydi family in Beit al-Ahmar village from the Sanhan clan, southeast of Sanaa. He joined the Royal Armed Forces in 1958 and participated in the 1962 military coup that established the Yemen Arab Republic. After President Ahmad al-Ghashmi was assassinated in 1978, Saleh was elected president of North Yemen on 17 July 1978. He survived multiple coup attempts and consolidated power through a patronage system involving his family and tribal allies. When North and South Yemen unified in 1990, Saleh became president of the new Republic of Yemen, defeating southern separatists in a 1994 civil war. He was re-elected in 1999 and 2006, ruling through increasingly autocratic means. During the 2011 Arab Spring, massive protests forced his resignation in 2012, and he was succeeded by Abdrabbuh Mansur Hadi. Saleh later allied with the Houthis during the Yemeni civil war but broke with them in December 2017. On 4 December 2017, he was killed by Houthi fighters while attempting to flee Sanaa.
achievements: President of North Yemen (1978-1990)
First President of unified Yemen (1990-2012)
Survived multiple coup attempts
Achieved Yemeni unification in 1990

figures.name: Ahmed Ali Abdullah Saleh al-Ahmar
figures.nameAr: أحمد علي عبد الله صالح الأحمر
figures.title: Former Republican Guard Commander
figures.roleDescription: Eldest son of Ali Abdullah Saleh and former military commander
figures.significance: Former commander of Yemen's Republican Guard and eldest son of long-serving president Ali Abdullah Saleh.
figures.biography: Ahmed Ali Abdullah Saleh al-Ahmar was born on July 25, 1972, the eldest son of former Yemeni President Ali Abdullah Saleh. His mother died when he was young, and before his father's resignation, he was widely seen as being groomed to eventually replace him as president. Ahmed commanded approximately 80,000 troops of the Republican Guard unit of the Yemeni Army and was involved in corruption through his ties to the Shibam Holding Company in 2008. After President Hadi came to power in 2012, Ahmed refused to relinquish control of long-range missiles to the Defense Ministry in December 2012. In response, President Hadi restructured the military in December 2012, effectively dissolving the Republican Guard and rendering Ahmed's position unnecessary. He was appointed as Yemen's Ambassador to the United Arab Emirates in May 2013, but was dismissed in March 2015 following the outbreak of the Yemeni Civil War. The UAE terminated his ambassadorship in April 2015 and he was reportedly placed under house arrest in Abu Dhabi. After his father was killed by the Houthis in 2017, Ahmed declared his animosity against them.
achievements: Commander of Republican Guard (80,000 troops)
Ambassador to United Arab Emirates (2013-2015)

figures.name: Amrullah Saleh
figures.title: First Vice President of Afghanistan
figures.roleDescription: Former head of Afghan intelligence and vice president
figures.significance: Former head of Afghanistan's National Directorate of Security who became first vice president and proclaimed himself caretaker president after the Taliban takeover in 2021.
figures.biography: Amrullah Saleh was born on 15 October 1972 in the Panjshir region of Afghanistan to an ethnic Tajik family. Orphaned at age 7, he joined the mujahideen in 1990 to avoid conscription and fought under Ahmad Shah Massoud. He became head of the Northern Alliance's international liaison office in Dushanbe, Tajikistan in 1997. After the Taliban's fall, he was appointed head of the National Directorate of Security (NDS) by President Hamid Karzai in December 2004. As intelligence chief, he initiated structural reforms, sent agents to infiltrate Taliban operations in Pakistan, and provided intelligence about bin Laden's possible location in Abbottabad in 2006. He resigned in June 2010 due to disagreements with Karzai over Taliban policy and founded the Basej-e Milli political movement. President Ashraf Ghani appointed him as acting Interior Minister in December 2018, and he became First Vice President in February 2020. After the Taliban takeover of Kabul on 15 August 2021, Saleh proclaimed himself caretaker president and supported the National Resistance Front in Panjshir Valley before fleeing to Tajikistan in September 2021.
achievements: Head of National Directorate of Security (2004-2010)
First Vice President of Afghanistan (2020-2021)
Founded Basej-e Milli political movement

figures.name: Aguila Saleh Issa Gueider
figures.title: Speaker of Libyan House of Representatives
figures.roleDescription: Libyan jurist and politician serving as parliamentary speaker
figures.significance: Speaker of Libya's House of Representatives who served as head of state during the second Libyan civil war from 2014-2021.
figures.biography: Aguila Saleh Issa Gueider was born on 11 January 1944 in the town of Al Qubbah, Libya. Following the 2014 Libyan parliamentary election, he was elected as Parliament Speaker but was forced to flee to Tobruk along with the rest of the Libyan House of Representatives after Tripoli was seized by militias. As Speaker, he effectively served as head of state of Libya from 2014 to 2021 under the Tobruk-based government, which was recognized by the international community as the legitimate government during the second Libyan civil war until 2016. On 20 February 2015, his residence was targeted in bombing by ISIL militants in Al Qubbah, resulting in at least 40 deaths in what became known as the Al Qubbah bombings. ISIL claimed the attacks were retaliation for Egyptian military intervention in Libya. On 20 September 2021, Aguila Saleh announced his intention to temporarily withdraw from parliament to run as a candidate for president in the 2021 Libyan general election.
achievements: Speaker of Libyan House of Representatives since 2014
Served as head of state during Libyan civil war (2014-2021)

figures.name: Abdul Elah al-Shaya
figures.title: Yemeni Journalist
figures.roleDescription: Journalist who interviewed Al-Qaeda figures
figures.significance: Yemeni journalist who conducted significant interviews with high-profile terrorist figures including Anwar al-Awlaki.
figures.biography:
Abdul Elah al-Shaya is a Yemeni journalist who gained international attention for his interviews with prominent terrorist figures. In December 2009, he reported on a U.S.-Yemeni airstrike in eastern Shabwa that was intended to target al-Qaeda members. Al-Shaya received a phone call from Anwar al-Awlaki on December 28, 2009, in which al-Awlaki reported that he was well and had not attended the al-Qaeda meeting that was targeted in the strike.

Al-Shaya played a significant role in providing information about al-Awlaki's activities and whereabouts during a critical period when the cleric was being hunted by Yemeni and U.S. authorities. He stated publicly that al-Awlaki was not tied to al-Qaeda, providing a contrasting perspective to official government positions. His journalistic work provided rare insights into the activities and statements of one of the most wanted terrorist figures of the time.
achievements: Conducted interviews with high-profile terrorist figures
Provided international media coverage of U.S.-Yemeni counterterrorism operations

figures.name: Uzi Shaya
figures.title: Former Israeli Intelligence Officer
figures.roleDescription: Former Mossad agent and counterterrorism official
figures.significance: Former Israeli Mossad agent who conducted counterterrorism operations and was involved in efforts to combat terrorist financing through Chinese banks.
figures.biography:
Uzi Shaya is a former Israeli intelligence officer who served as a counterterrorism official with the Mossad. He conducted extensive counterterrorism operations, including traveling to China dozens of times in his official capacity to work on disrupting terrorist financing networks. Shaya was particularly involved in Israeli efforts to combat terrorist financing through Bank of China accounts that were allegedly being used to fund terrorist activities.

Following the 2006 Tel Aviv shawarma restaurant bombing that killed American teenager Daniel Wultz, the Israeli government initially supported the Wultz family's legal case against Bank of China and was prepared to allow Shaya to testify about his knowledge of Israeli-Chinese meetings regarding terrorist financing. However, after Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's 2013 state visit to Beijing, Israeli policy changed, and the government prevented Shaya from testifying in U.S. court proceedings, claiming his testimony would jeopardize Israeli national security interests. U.S. District Judge Shira Scheindlin ultimately quashed a subpoena against Shaya, ruling that Israel had not waived his immunity regarding information he learned in his official capacity.
achievements: Conducted extensive counterterrorism operations for Israeli intelligence
Led efforts to combat terrorist financing networks in China

figures.name: Shafik Wazzan
figures.title: Former Prime Minister of Lebanon
figures.roleDescription: Lebanese political leader who served as Prime Minister
figures.significance: Former Prime Minister of Lebanon who survived a car bomb attack in late 1991.
figures.biography: Shafik Wazzan served as Prime Minister of Lebanon during the tumultuous period of the Lebanese Civil War. He was a prominent Sunni Muslim political figure who held the highest executive office in the country. In late December 1991, Wazzan was the target of a significant assassination attempt when a car bomb, estimated at 220 pounds of TNT, exploded in the Muslim neighborhood of Basta. The attack killed at least thirty people and wounded 120 others. Remarkably, Wazzan survived the attack because he was riding in a bulletproof car at the time of the explosion. This incident occurred during the post-civil war period when Lebanon was still experiencing sporadic violence despite the official end of hostilities in 1990. The attack on Wazzan highlighted the continued dangers facing Lebanese political leaders even after the implementation of the amnesty law in March 1991.
achievements: Served as Prime Minister of Lebanon
Survived assassination attempt in 1991

figures.name: Shams al-Mulk Abu'l-Fath Tahir ibn Wazzan
figures.nameAr: شمس الملك أبو الفتح طاهر بن وزان
figures.title: Former Vizier of the Fatimid Caliphate
figures.roleDescription: High-ranking administrative official in the Fatimid government
figures.significance: Served as vizier of the Fatimid Caliphate before being replaced by al-Rudhabari in 1024.
figures.biography: Shams al-Mulk Abu'l-Fath Tahir ibn Wazzan held the prestigious position of vizier in the Fatimid Caliphate during the early 11th century. The vizierate was one of the most important administrative positions in the Fatimid state, serving as the chief minister to the caliph and overseeing the various government departments (diwans). Ibn Wazzan's tenure as vizier came to an end in 1024 during the reign of Caliph al-Zahir (r. 1021–1036). He was replaced by al-Rudhabari, who was appointed under the general direction of the diwan. The Fatimid vizierate during this period was characterized by frequent changes in leadership, often reflecting the political instability and court intrigues that marked al-Zahir's reign. Ibn Wazzan's dismissal was part of the broader administrative reorganization that occurred during the early years of al-Zahir's caliphate, as the young caliph and his advisors sought to consolidate power and manage the various challenges facing the Fatimid state.
achievements: Served as vizier of the Fatimid Caliphate
Held high administrative position during al-Zahir's reign

figures.name: Maha Al-Ghunaim
figures.title: Businesswoman and Co-founder of Global Investment House
figures.roleDescription: Chair and Managing Director of GIH (Global Investment House)
figures.significance: Co-founded and led one of Kuwait's major investment firms, managing billions in assets across international markets.
figures.biography: Maha Al-Ghunaim is a prominent Kuwaiti businesswoman who graduated from San Francisco State University in 1982. Earlier in her career, she served as assistant general manager of asset management at the Kuwait Investment Company. In 1998, she co-founded GIH (Global Investment House), which became a significant player in the regional investment landscape. Under her leadership as chair and managing director, GIH was listed on the London Stock Exchange in 2008. By 2006, GIH managed approximately $7 billion in assets, offering a variety of different funds and venturing into investments with India, China and Turkey. In 2005, GIH was responsible for aiding Lebanese telecom Investcom's establishment in London and Dubai with $200 million in funds. According to Bloomberg, she served as global chairperson of GIH from March 2007 to September 2014, until the closure of the firm on April 17, 2017. In 2010, she was named 94th in Bloomberg's Power Women 100 list. She also heads the Kuwaiti chapter of Young Arab Leaders, a pan-Arab organization which aims to develop education and entrepreneurship in Kuwait.
achievements: Co-founded Global Investment House (GIH) in 1998
Led GIH's listing on London Stock Exchange in 2008
Managed $7 billion in assets through GIH by 2006

figures.biography: Dr. Ghadah G. Al-Ghunaim serves as a member of the Board of Trustees at the King Abdulaziz Center for National Dialogue (KACND), an important Saudi institution established in 2003. The Center was created to promote intellectual dialogue between Saudi citizens and different segments of Saudi society, with the goal of strengthening national unity and combating extremism. As a board member, she participates in overseeing the Center's mission to establish and disseminate the culture of dialogue in society, discuss national issues affecting Saudi citizens, and enable participation of different groups and intellectual tendencies through dialogue mechanisms. The Center organizes annual National Dialogue Forums in different cities across Saudi Arabia, addressing topics ranging from national unity to youth expectations, women's rights, education, and media relations.

figures.significance: Served as mayor of Tehran from 1961 to 1963 and was the youngest man ever appointed to the post at that time.
figures.biography:
Ahmad Nafisi was an Iranian bureaucrat who served as mayor of Tehran from 1961 to 1963. He was notable for being the youngest man ever appointed to this position at that time. He was married to Nezhat Nafisi, who became a member of the first group of women elected to the National Consultative Assembly in 1963.

Ahmad Nafisi was the father of Azar Nafisi, the renowned Iranian-American writer and professor who authored 'Reading Lolita in Tehran.' His family background provided the foundation for his daughter's later academic and literary career, as she grew up in a politically engaged household during a significant period in Iranian history.

figures.title: Politician and Women's Rights Pioneer
figures.roleDescription: Iranian politician and member of the National Consultative Assembly
figures.significance: Member of the first group of women elected to Iran's National Consultative Assembly in 1963, pioneering women's political participation in Iran.
figures.biography:
Nezhat Nafisi was an Iranian politician and pioneering advocate for women's rights who made history in 1963 when she became a member of the first group of women elected to the National Consultative Assembly of Iran. This achievement marked a significant milestone in Iranian women's political participation and representation.

She was married to Ahmad Nafisi, who served as mayor of Tehran from 1961 to 1963, making them a prominent political couple in Iranian society. Nezhat was the mother of Azar Nafisi, who would later become a renowned Iranian-American writer and academic. Her groundbreaking role in Iranian politics helped pave the way for future generations of women in public service and demonstrated the potential for women's meaningful participation in the country's governance.
achievements: First group of women elected to Iran's National Consultative Assembly (1963)

figures.name: Amna Mahmoud Al-Jaidah
figures.title: First Woman Teacher and Principal in Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Pioneer educator who established Qatar's first school for girls
figures.significance: She was the first woman teacher in Qatar and opened the first school for girls in Qatar, becoming the first woman to lead a school in 1953.
figures.biography:
Amna Mahmoud Al-Jaidah was born in 1913 to Mahmoud Youssef Al-Jaydah, a pearl diver and seller, and Nayela Bint Mohamed Darwish. Her father encouraged her education, believing both boys and girls required good schooling. At age seven, she began memorizing the Quran under Sheikh Hamid Bin Ahmed Bin Mohamed, completing this unusual achievement for a female in three years and becoming a Hafiz. She began teaching at fourteen, instructing women older than herself, including her sister and aunt, from her home as girls were forbidden from traveling to other houses without an escort.

After four years of marriage in Bahrain from age eighteen, during which she had two children, she returned to Qatar around 1938 at age 25 and expanded her teaching. Her school became known as 'Amna Mahmoud's school' and grew in popularity, teaching not only the Quran but also reading and writing. In 1953, her school came under the direction of the Qatari Ministry of Education, making it the first official school for girls and making her the first woman teacher paid by the state. She became the country's first woman Principal of what was named the Banat El Doha School (Daughters of Doha School), later serving as principal of multiple girls' schools until her retirement in 1976.
achievements: First woman teacher in Qatar

figures.name: Ibrahim Jaidah
figures.title: Renowned Qatari Architect
figures.roleDescription: Architect who designed major Qatari landmarks
figures.significance: He is a renowned Qatari architect who designed the state mosque Mohammed Bin Abdul Wahab mosque and Al-Thumama Stadium for the 2022 FIFA World Cup.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim Jaidah is a renowned Qatari architect who has made significant contributions to Qatar's architectural landscape. He designed the Mohammed Bin Abdul Wahab mosque, which serves as Qatar's state mosque, located in the Lejbailat neighbourhood of Doha. The mosque's design draws on traditional Qatari architecture, reflecting the country's cultural heritage in its architectural form.

Jaidah also designed Al-Thumama Stadium, a football stadium located in the Al Thumama district of Doha, approximately 13 km south of the city center. The stadium hosted matches during the 2022 FIFA World Cup held in Qatar. His architectural design for the stadium takes its inspiration from the traditional taqiyah hat, a traditional cap worn by men and boys across the Middle East, demonstrating his ability to blend traditional cultural elements with modern sporting architecture.
achievements: Designed Qatar's state mosque (Mohammed Bin Abdul Wahab mosque)
Designed Al-Thumama Stadium for 2022 FIFA World Cup

figures.biography: Mohammed bin Ismail Al-Emadi is a prominent member of the Al-Emadi family who has served in multiple capacities for the Qatari government. He has held positions as a Qatari Ambassador, politician, and businessman, representing the family's involvement in Qatar's diplomatic and political spheres.

figures.name: Mohamad Reslan Al-Khayyat
figures.title: Founder of Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the Al-Khayyat family business empire
figures.significance: Established the foundational family business in 1983 that would later grow into a major Qatari business conglomerate.
figures.biography:
Mohamad Reslan Al-Khayyat was the patriarch who established the Al-Khayyat family's business presence in Qatar. In 1983, he founded Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading, which became the foundational family business from which the Al-Khayyat business empire would grow.

His sons Ramez Al-Khayyat and Moutaz Al-Khayyat would later build upon this foundation, with Ramez starting his career as a board member of the family company before expanding into larger ventures. The business legacy established by Mohamad Reslan Al-Khayyat eventually grew into Power International Holding, one of Qatar's major conglomerates operating across multiple sectors including construction, real estate, food and agriculture, hospitality, and services.
achievements: Founded Al-Khayyat Contracting and Trading in 1983

figures.name: Ali bin Rashed bin Mubarak Al Khater
figures.title: Sheikh and Ruler of Al Wakrah
figures.roleDescription: Historical ruler of Al Wakrah, former capital of Qatar
figures.significance: Ruler of Al Wakrah, which was built by the Al Khater family and served as Qatar's former capital.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Ali bin Rashed bin Mubarak Al Khater served as the ruler of Al Wakrah, one of the two major cities built by the Al Khater family in the Arabian Peninsula. Al Wakrah held particular significance as it served as the former capital of Qatar before the establishment of Doha as the current capital. As ruler of this important city, Ali bin Rashed played a crucial role in the early political and economic development of what would become modern Qatar.

His leadership of Al Wakrah came during a period when the Al Khater family wielded considerable influence in the region, with close political and family ties to the Al Thani ruling family of Qatar, the Al Khalifa rulers of Bahrain, and the Al Saud family of Saudi Arabia. The Al Khater family's construction and governance of Al Wakrah demonstrates their significant contribution to the urban and political development of the Gulf region during this formative period.
achievements: Ruler of Al Wakrah, former capital of Qatar
Led one of the major cities built by the Al Khater family
Contributed to early political development of Qatar

figures.name: Abdulla bin Ali Al Khater
figures.title: Sheikh and Ruler of Al Wakrah
figures.roleDescription: Ruler of Al Wakrah and co-founder of Jubail
figures.significance: Served as ruler of Al Wakrah and was one of three founders of Jubail city in Saudi Arabia.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdulla bin Ali Al Khater held the important position of ruler of Al Wakrah, the former capital of Qatar, demonstrating the Al Khater family's significant role in the early governance of what would become modern Qatar. Beyond his role in Qatar, he was also one of the three founding figures of Jubail, one of the major cities in Saudi Arabia built by the Al Khater family.

As both a ruler and city founder, Abdulla bin Ali Al Khater exemplified the Al Khater family's extensive influence across the Arabian Peninsula during the formative period of modern Gulf states. His dual role in both Qatar and Saudi Arabia reflects the family's broad regional presence and their contribution to urban development and political leadership in multiple territories. The founding of Jubail and governance of Al Wakrah represent significant achievements in the historical development of the Gulf region's major urban centers.
achievements: Ruler of Al Wakrah, former capital of Qatar
One of three founders of Jubail city in Saudi Arabia
Contributed to urban development in both Qatar and Saudi Arabia

figures.name: Mubarak bin Ali Al Khater
figures.title: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs
figures.roleDescription: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs in Qatar
figures.significance: Served as Qatar's Minister of Foreign Affairs, representing the Al Khater family's continued role in Qatari government.
figures.biography:
Mubarak bin Ali Al Khater served as Qatar's Minister of Foreign Affairs, representing the Al Khater family's longstanding tradition of diplomatic and governmental service in the modern state of Qatar. His appointment to this crucial ministerial position demonstrates the continued trust and respect the Al Thani ruling family placed in the Al Khater family for important governmental roles.

As Minister of Foreign Affairs, Mubarak bin Ali would have been responsible for Qatar's international relations and diplomatic initiatives during his tenure. His role exemplifies how the Al Khater family transitioned from their historical roles as city builders and regional power brokers to serving in key positions within Qatar's modern governmental structure, maintaining their influence and contributing to the nation's diplomatic efforts in the contemporary era.
achievements: Served as Qatar's Minister of Foreign Affairs
Represented Al Khater family in high-level government position
Contributed to Qatar's diplomatic relations

figures.name: Mohammed bin Khater Al Khater
figures.title: Ambassador to India
figures.roleDescription: Former Ambassador to Indonesia and current Ambassador to India
figures.significance: Represents Qatar's diplomatic interests as Ambassador to India and previously served in Indonesia.
figures.biography:
Mohammed bin Khater Al Khater has served Qatar in important diplomatic positions, demonstrating the Al Khater family's continued prominence in Qatar's foreign relations. He previously served as Qatar's Ambassador to Indonesia, where he would have been responsible for strengthening bilateral relations between Qatar and one of Southeast Asia's most important nations.

Currently, Mohammed bin Khater Al Khater serves as Qatar's Ambassador to India, a crucial diplomatic position given India's significant role in South Asian geopolitics and its large expatriate population in Qatar. His appointment to represent Qatar in India reflects both his diplomatic expertise and the continued trust placed in the Al Khater family for managing Qatar's most important international relationships. His career exemplifies how the family has maintained its influential role in Qatar's modern diplomatic apparatus.
achievements: Current Ambassador of Qatar to India
Former Ambassador of Qatar to Indonesia
Represents Qatar in key diplomatic relationships in Asia

figures.name: Khalil Al-Mohannadi
figures.title: ITTF Deputy President
figures.roleDescription: International table tennis administrator
figures.significance: Deputy President of the International Table Tennis Federation (ITTF) and member of the World Table Tennis selection panel.
figures.biography:
Khalil Al-Mohannadi serves as the Deputy President of the International Table Tennis Federation (ITTF), representing Qatar in international sports administration beyond football. He was appointed to the selection panel for World Table Tennis (WTT) in 2019, a new commercial vehicle for table tennis created by the ITTF. The selection panel, which included ITTF President Thomas Weikert and other senior officials, was tasked with launching WTT to commercialize professional table tennis globally.

As Deputy President of ITTF, Al-Mohannadi plays a crucial role in the governance and development of table tennis worldwide. His involvement in the WTT selection panel demonstrates his influence in modernizing and commercializing the sport, working alongside other international sports administrators to enhance table tennis's global appeal and professional structure.
achievements: Deputy President of International Table Tennis Federation (ITTF)
Member of World Table Tennis (WTT) selection panel

figures.name: Hamad Lahan Al Mohannadi
figures.title: Municipal Council Representative
figures.roleDescription: Elected representative for Al Thakhira constituency
figures.significance: Elected municipal representative for Al Thakhira constituency in 2011 and retained the position in 2015.
figures.biography:
Hamad Lahan Al Mohannadi became the municipal representative for Al Thakhira constituency when he was elected in the 2011 Central Municipal Council elections, taking over from Ali Hassan Al-Mohannadi who had served for three consecutive terms. He successfully retained his position in the 2015 elections, demonstrating continued support from the local community.

During his tenure, Al Thakhira constituency (which became constituency no. 26 in 2015) included parts of Simaisma, Umm Birka, and Ras Laffan in addition to Al Thakhira itself. His representation coincided with significant development in the region, including infrastructure improvements and heritage preservation projects that were implemented in Al Thakhira during the 2010s.
achievements: Elected to Al Thakhira municipal council seat (2011)
Retained municipal council position (2015)
Represented constituency including Al Thakhira, Simaisma, Umm Birka, and Ras Laffan

figures.name: Rashid Jassim Al-Mohannadi
figures.title: Municipal Council Representative
figures.roleDescription: Elected representative for Al Khor constituency
figures.significance: First elected representative for Al Khor constituency in Qatar's inaugural municipal elections in 1999.
figures.biography:
Rashid Jassim Al-Mohannadi was elected as the first municipal representative for Al Khor constituency (no. 26) in Qatar's inaugural Central Municipal Council elections in 1999. He won decisively with 63.3% of the vote (283 votes), significantly ahead of runner-up Saleh Majed Al-Mohannadi who received 12.3% (54 votes). The voter turnout in Al Khor constituency was 83.4%, reflecting strong community participation.

Al-Mohannadi successfully retained his seat in the 2002 elections, serving during a period of significant development in Al Khor as the city modernized and expanded its infrastructure. His constituency included not only Al Khor city but also areas such as Simaisma, Al Daayen Village, north Lusail, and Ras Matbakh, making him responsible for representing a diverse range of communities in northern Qatar.
achievements: Won inaugural Qatar municipal elections for Al Khor constituency (1999)
Retained municipal council seat in 2002 elections
Received decisive victory with 63.3% of votes in 1999

figures.name: Saqer Saeed Al-Mohannadi
figures.title: Municipal Council Representative
figures.roleDescription: Elected representative for Al Khor constituency
figures.significance: Municipal representative for Al Khor constituency elected in 2007 and retained the position in 2011.
figures.biography:
Saqer Saeed Al-Mohannadi was elected to represent Al Khor constituency in the 2007 Central Municipal Council elections, taking over from Rashid Jassim Al-Mohannadi. He successfully retained his position in the 2011 elections, demonstrating sustained support from the Al Khor community during a period of significant growth and development in the city.

During his tenure representing constituency no. 26 (later no. 25), Al-Mohannadi oversaw municipal governance during Al Khor's expansion related to the nearby Ras Laffan Industrial City and the city's preparation for hosting major sporting events. His constituency included Al Khor city as well as surrounding areas such as Simaisma, Al Daayen Village, north Lusail, and Ras Matbakh.
achievements: Elected to Al Khor municipal council seat (2007)
Retained municipal council position (2011)
Represented Al Khor during period of significant urban development

figures.name: Mohammed Ali Al Mohannadi
figures.title: Cultural Club Founder
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Al Ittihad cultural club in Al Khor
figures.significance: Founded Al Najma cultural club in 1966 (later renamed Al Ittihad), one of three major social and cultural clubs in Al Khor.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Ali Al Mohannadi was a cultural pioneer in Al Khor who founded the Al Najma cultural club in 1966, which was later renamed Al Ittihad. This was one of three significant social and cultural clubs established in Al Khor by the 1960s, alongside Al Asifah and Al Kifah, reflecting the city's growing cultural sophistication during the early oil era.

The club operated a theater in a residential house where they hosted plays addressing various social issues, charging admission fees to attendees. Beyond theatrical performances, Al Ittihad also organized amateur football matches and other cultural activities. Mohammed Ali's initiative in establishing this cultural institution helped foster artistic expression and community engagement in Al Khor during a transformative period in Qatar's history.
achievements: Founded Al Najma cultural club (1966, later renamed Al Ittihad)
Established theater programming addressing social issues
Organized cultural performances and amateur football matches

figures.name: Issa bin Ali Abu Jamhour Al Mohannadi
figures.title: Merchant and Contractor
figures.roleDescription: Founder of first local contracting company in Al Thakhira
figures.significance: Founded the first local contracting company in Al Thakhira in the 1930s, responsible for building many local houses, wells and mosques.
figures.biography:
Issa bin Ali Abu Jamhour Al Mohannadi was a pioneering businessman who established the first local contracting company in Al Thakhira in the 1930s, during the early period of Qatar's modernization. His company played a crucial role in the development of Al Thakhira's infrastructure, taking responsibility for constructing many of the village's essential buildings and facilities.

His contracting company was instrumental in building numerous local houses, wells, and mosques throughout Al Thakhira, contributing significantly to the community's physical development during a time when such local expertise was rare. His entrepreneurial initiative helped establish indigenous construction capabilities in the region, reducing dependence on external contractors and fostering local economic development during the pre-oil era.
achievements: Founded first local contracting company in Al Thakhira (1930s)
Built numerous houses, wells and mosques in the village
Pioneered local construction industry in the region

figures.name: Munira Fakhro
figures.title: Dr.
figures.roleDescription: Academic and political activist
figures.significance: Associate professor at University of Bahrain and prominent women's rights advocate who was a candidate for Wa'ad party in 2006 elections.
figures.biography:
Dr. Munira Fakhro is an associate professor at the University of Bahrain, having received her Doctorate in Social Policy, Planning and Administration from Columbia University where she has served as a visiting scholar since 1997. She has conducted research on gender, citizenship and civil society in the Persian Gulf states at the Centre for Middle East Studies, Harvard University, and has published works on Bahrain including issues related to women, civil society and democratisation.

During the 1990s uprising, Dr. Fakhro was a signatory to the 1994 Popular Petition to the Amir calling for the restoration of the parliament and the 1973 constitution, followed by another petition in 1995 signed by 350 women calling for the restoration of democracy and an end to torture. As a result, the government demanded she withdraw her name or be sacked from her university position. She refused and was dismissed along with other signatories, but was later restored to her job when King Hamad carried out a general amnesty. In 2006, she ran for parliament as a candidate for Wa'ad, the country's largest leftist political party. She is a cousin of Layla Fakhro and currently serves as a board member of the Bahrain Academic Society and the Supreme Council for Women.
achievements: Doctorate from Columbia University
Visiting scholar at Columbia since 1997
Research at Harvard's Centre for Middle East Studies
Signatory to 1994 and 1995 pro-democracy petitions
2006 parliamentary candidate for Wa'ad party

figures.name: Abdulla bin Yousif Fakhro
figures.title: Business Leader
figures.roleDescription: Second-generation leader of Fakhro Group
figures.significance: Led the major expansion of Fakhro Group after his father's death, transforming it into a diversified conglomerate with 14 companies.
figures.biography:
Abdulla bin Yousif Fakhro was one of the five sons of founder Yousif bin Abdulrahman Fakhro. During the 1930s and 1940s, he was sent by his father to manage the family's business interests in Iraq, where he was based for numerous years. After Yousif bin Abdulrahman's death in 1952, Abdulla and his brothers jointly controlled the family business.

Abdulla later acquired most of the businesses from his brothers and, with the help of his sons, embarked on an ambitious growth strategy that transformed the group into what it is today. Under his leadership, the group expanded to include interests in automotive products and services, restaurants, shipping logistics, insurance, and IT services. The group currently consists of 14 individual companies and operates in Bahrain, Qatar, Dubai, and Abu Dhabi, with plans for expansion into Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.
achievements: Managed family business interests in Iraq for numerous years
Acquired majority control of family businesses from his brothers
Expanded group to 14 companies across multiple sectors
Extended operations to Qatar, UAE with plans for Saudi Arabia and Kuwait expansion

figures.name: Layla Abdulla Yousif Fakhro
figures.title: Educator and Revolutionary
figures.roleDescription: Revolutionary educator and political activist
figures.significance: Participated in the Dhofar Rebellion in Oman and established the first school of the revolution, becoming a legend throughout the Arab world.
figures.biography:
Layla Abdulla Yousif Fakhro was born in 1945 in Muharraq island in Bahrain. She held a master's degree in statistics from the American University of Beirut, Lebanon and a license in statistics from Al-Muntassiria University in Baghdad, Iraq. She became involved in political activities in Bahrain in 1964 and was a student leader and activist while studying in Beirut in the 1960s, serving as head of the cultural committee from 1967-1968.

Fakhro took part in the Dhofar Rebellion in Oman in the 1960s under the assumed name 'Huda Salem', where she established the first school of the revolution. Much of the top echelon of Omani government and enterprise passed through this school, providing the basis for the modern educational system in Oman. She also established the Awal Women's Society in Bahrain in 1968. In 1983, she moved with her twin daughters Munira and Aisha to Nicosia, Cyprus, to spend more than ten years in exile, during which she established the Dilmun publishing house. She returned to Bahrain in 1995 and established Alnadeem Information Technology company. She died after a long illness on 21 September 2006, survived by her daughters Munira and Aysha, and husband Ubaiydli Al-Ubaiydli.
achievements: Participated in Dhofar Rebellion in Oman
Established first school of the revolution in Oman
Founded Awal Women's Society in 1968
Established Dilmun Publishing House in Cyprus
Founded Alnadeem Information Technology company

figures.name: Mohamed bin Abdulrahman M. Hassan Fakhro
figures.nameAr: محمد بن عبدالرحمن بن محمد بن حسن بن محمود آل فخرو
figures.title: Half of the World (نص الدنيا)
figures.roleDescription: Wealthy businessman and trusted advisor to Qatari rulers
figures.significance: Wealthy businessman from Qatar who fought in the Zubara War of 1935 and served as trusted advisor to Qatari rulers, nicknamed 'Half of the World' for his wisdom.
figures.biography:
Mohamed Bin Abdulrahman Al-Hassan Al-Fakhro, nicknamed 'Half of the World' (نص الدنيا), was a wealthy businessman from Qatar who belonged to the Al-Hassan branch of the Al-Fakhro family, attributed to the Banu-Tamim tribe. He fought in the Zubara War of 1935 with the Al Thani and was given his nickname by the then ruler of Qatar, Sheikh Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani, for his vast knowledge and wisdom. Sheikh Abdullah considered him his right-hand man, and it was known that on average 4 times a week the Sheikh would have his afternoon naps in Bayt Fakhro.

He was said to have brought two cannons from India on-board his uncle's ships after smuggling gold coins sewn into his vest, as it was forbidden by the English to smuggle gold out of the Persian Gulf area. In 1948, he moved to live in Bahrain due to his second marriage to the daughter of Yousif bin Abdulrahman Fakhro. Later in life, he was in charge of overseeing the education of the sons and nephews of Sheikh Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani, including future rulers Sheikh Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani and Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani. He had nine sons, including Dr. Ali (Former Minister of Health and Education in Bahrain) and Jamal (Deputy Chairman Shura Council - Bahrain).
achievements: Fought in Zubara War of 1935
Served as right-hand man to Sheikh Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani
Smuggled cannons from India for defense
Oversaw education of Qatari royal family members
Father of multiple prominent figures including ministers

figures.name: Connie Kay Nass
figures.title: Indiana State Auditor
figures.roleDescription: American Republican politician who served as Indiana State Auditor
figures.significance: Served as Indiana State Auditor from 1999 to 2007 after holding various local political offices.
figures.biography:
Connie Kay Nass is an American Republican politician from Indiana who built her career through various local and state positions. She began her political career in 1980 when she was elected to the Huntingburg Common Council, serving two terms. In 1987, she successfully ran for mayor of Huntingburg, defeating six-term Democratic incumbent Dale Helmerich and serving two terms until 1996 when she was succeeded by Republican Gail Kemp.

Nass went on to serve as Indiana State Auditor from 1999 to 2007, an eight-year tenure that was sometimes controversial as Democrats accused her of 'injecting partisan rhetoric' into what was typically considered a neutral role due to her criticisms of Democratic governor Frank O'Bannon. After retiring from politics in 2007, she served as chairwoman of Indiana's Abraham Lincoln Bicentennial Commission. Throughout her career, she received several honors including the Protect Our Woods Award in 1990, an honorary doctorate in humanities from Oakland City University in 2005, and the Sagamore of the Wabash from Indiana governor Mitch Daniels in 2006. She and her husband Alan have three children and operate a funeral home called Nass & Son.
achievements: Served as Indiana State Auditor for 8 years (1999-2007)
Elected mayor of Huntingburg, defeating six-term incumbent
Received Sagamore of the Wabash award from Governor Mitch Daniels
Served as chairwoman of Indiana's Abraham Lincoln Bicentennial Commission

figures.name: Marieke Wijen-Nass
figures.title: Member of Dutch House of Representatives
figures.roleDescription: Dutch politician for the Farmer–Citizen Movement
figures.significance: Served as a member of the Dutch House of Representatives representing the Farmer–Citizen Movement from July 2024 to November 2025.
figures.biography:
Marieke Wijen-Nass was born on March 10, 1993, and became a Dutch politician representing the Farmer–Citizen Movement (BoerBurgerBeweging). She served as a member of the House of Representatives of the Netherlands between July 2024 and November 2025, succeeding Gijs Tuinman who had been appointed defence state secretary in the Schoof cabinet. Her political portfolio included defense, justice, security, housing, and spatial planning.

During her time in the House of Representatives, Wijen-Nass held several committee assignments including vice chair positions on the Committee for Digital Affairs and the Committee for Housing and Spatial Planning. She also served on committees for the Interior, Defence, and Credentials, as well as the Contact group France. In an October 2024 debate, she expressed support for Ukraine in its defense against the Russian invasion but opposed Ukrainian membership in NATO afterwards, arguing that Ukraine should serve as a buffer state between Russia and NATO countries.
achievements: Served as member of Dutch House of Representatives (2024-2025)
Held vice chair positions on multiple parliamentary committees
Represented Farmer–Citizen Movement in national politics

figures.name: Wilfred Nass Sr.
figures.title: Police Officer
figures.roleDescription: Longtime police officer in Whitewater, Wisconsin
figures.significance: Father of Wisconsin politician Stephen Nass and longtime law enforcement officer in Whitewater.
figures.biography: Wilfred Nass Sr. was a longtime police officer in Whitewater, Wisconsin, and the father of four children including future Wisconsin politician Stephen Leonard Nass. He was married to Joyce (née Sherman) and the family were descendants of Friedrich Nass who had settled in Walworth County, Wisconsin, in the 1880s after emigrating from the Pomerania region of the German Empire. Wilfred Nass Sr. served the Whitewater community through his career in law enforcement, establishing the family's presence in the area that would later see his son Stephen become deeply involved in Wisconsin politics, serving on the Whitewater city council and eventually in the state legislature.
achievements: Served as longtime police officer in Whitewater, Wisconsin
Father of Wisconsin State Senator Stephen Nass

figures.name: Friedrich Nass
figures.title: Immigrant Settler
figures.roleDescription: German immigrant who settled in Wisconsin
figures.significance: Founding patriarch of the Nass family in Wisconsin, emigrating from Germany in the 1880s.
figures.biography: Friedrich Nass was a German immigrant who settled in Walworth County, Wisconsin, in the 1880s after emigrating from the Pomerania region of the German Empire. He established the Nass family presence in Wisconsin, becoming the ancestor of future generations including Wisconsin politician Stephen Leonard Nass. His decision to immigrate to Wisconsin during the late 19th century was part of the larger wave of German immigration to the American Midwest, where many German families sought new opportunities and established communities that would endure for generations. The Nass family line he established would eventually produce notable figures in Wisconsin politics and public service.
achievements: Established the Nass family presence in Wisconsin
Emigrated from Pomerania region of German Empire in 1880s

figures.name: Urwah ibn Zubayr
figures.title: Member of the Seven Fuqaha of Medina
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Islamic jurist, hadith scholar, and historian
figures.significance: One of the Seven Fuqaha of Medina and the first writer of Seerah (biographical accounts of Prophet Muhammad).
figures.biography:
Urwah ibn Zubayr was the youngest son of Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and Asma bint Abi Bakr. He became one of the most influential jurists of his time, being counted among the Seven Fuqaha of Medina, the most prominent group of Islamic legal scholars of the early period. Urwah was also a distinguished hadith scholar who contributed significantly to the preservation and transmission of Islamic traditions.

Urwah is particularly noted as the first writer of Seerah or Maghazi (biographical and military accounts of Prophet Muhammad), making him a pioneering figure in Islamic historiography. His scholarly work laid important foundations for later Islamic jurisprudence and historical writing. He lived through the major political upheavals of his time, including his brother Abdullah's caliphate and the Umayyad restoration.
achievements: Member of the Seven Fuqaha of Medina
First writer of Seerah (Prophet's biography)
Prominent hadith scholar
Pioneer in Islamic historiography

figures.name: Mus'ab ibn al-Zubayr
figures.title: Governor of Basra
figures.roleDescription: Military commander and governor representing his brother's caliphate in Iraq
figures.significance: Served as his brother Abdullah's governor of Basra and Iraq, maintaining Zubayrid authority in the east until his death.
figures.biography:
Mus'ab ibn al-Zubayr was a son of Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and brother of the caliph Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr. When his brother proclaimed himself caliph, Mus'ab was appointed as governor of Basra and its dependencies, effectively ruling Iraq on behalf of the Zubayrid caliphate. He proved to be a capable military commander and administrator, successfully maintaining his brother's authority in the strategically important Iraqi provinces.

Mus'ab's most significant military achievement was defeating and killing the revolutionary leader Mukhtar al-Thaqafi in April 687, who had challenged Zubayrid authority in Kufa. However, he was temporarily dismissed by his brother around 686-687 due to administrative issues but was reinstated in 687-688. Mus'ab's rule in Iraq came to an end in 691 when he was defeated and killed by Umayyad forces under Abd al-Malik at the Battle of Maskin, marking a crucial turning point in the Second Fitna.
achievements: Governor of Basra and Iraq under Zubayrid caliphate
Defeated and killed Mukhtar al-Thaqafi
Maintained Zubayrid authority in eastern provinces

figures.name: Ahmed Al-Zubair al-Senussi
figures.title: Prince of the Senussi House
figures.roleDescription: Libyan political figure and member of National Transitional Council
figures.significance: Libyan prince who spent 31 years as a political prisoner under Gaddafi, becoming the longest incarcerated prisoner in modern Libyan history.
figures.biography:
Prince Ahmed Al-Zubair al-Senussi was born in 1934 as a grandcousin of King Idris of Libya and nephew of Queen Fatimah. He graduated from the Military Academy of Iraq in 1958 and married his wife Fatilah in 1961. In 1970, he began planning to overthrow Muammar Gaddafi, who had seized power the previous year, allegedly seeking to give people a choice between monarchy or constitutional republic.

Ahmed was arrested and sentenced to death, though this was later commuted to additional imprisonment. He spent 31 years as a political prisoner, including nine years in solitary confinement, during which he claims to have been frequently tortured. His wife died while he was in captivity. He was finally released in 2001 on the 32nd anniversary of Gaddafi's coup, making him the longest incarcerated prisoner in modern Libyan history. In 2011, he won the European Parliament's Sakharov Prize for Freedom of Thought and in 2012 was announced as leader of the self-declared Cyrenaica Transitional Council.
achievements: Longest incarcerated political prisoner in modern Libyan history
Winner of 2011 Sakharov Prize for Freedom of Thought
Leader of Cyrenaica Transitional Council
Member of National Transitional Council

figures.name: Al-Zubayr ibn Abd al-Muttalib
figures.title: Uncle of Prophet Muhammad
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Hilf al-Fudul and guardian of young Muhammad
figures.significance: Founded the Hilf al-Fudul order of chivalry and served as co-guardian of the young Prophet Muhammad.
figures.biography:
Al-Zubayr ibn Abd al-Muttalib was the son of Abd al-Muttalib and Fatima bint Amr, making him an uncle of Prophet Muhammad and full brother to Abu Talib and Abdullah (Muhammad's father). He married Atika bint Abi Wahb of the Makhzum clan and had four daughters and one son. After the death of his eldest half-brother Al-Harith, Al-Zubayr took responsibility with Abu Talib for providing food and drink for pilgrims.

Al-Zubayr is most notable for founding the Hilf al-Fudul, an order of chivalry formed in Mecca in May 591 for the suppression of violence and injustice. This pact aimed to protect strangers and prevent wrongdoing in Mecca. He also shared joint guardianship of the young Muhammad and is said to have taken him on a journey to Yemen in 584 CE. Al-Zubayr participated in the rebuilding of the Kaaba in 605 and composed poetry describing the miraculous removal of a snake by an eagle that had prevented the construction work.
achievements: Founded the Hilf al-Fudul order of chivalry
Co-guardian of young Prophet Muhammad
Participated in rebuilding of the Kaaba
Provided for pilgrims in Mecca

figures.name: Larry Towell
figures.title: Photographer and Photojournalist
figures.roleDescription: Canadian photographer, poet, and oral historian specializing in conflict photography
figures.significance: Became the only Canadian member of Magnum Photos in 1988 and is renowned for his photographs of political conflict zones worldwide.
figures.biography:
Larry Towell was born in 1953 in Chatham-Kent, Ontario, and grew up in a large family in rural Ontario. He studied visual arts at York University in Toronto, where his interest in photography first began under teacher Tim Whiten. In 1976, he volunteered to work in Calcutta, India, where he became interested in questions about wealth distribution and issues of land and landlessness.

Towell became a freelance photographer in 1984 and joined Magnum Photos in 1988, becoming the only Canadian associated with the prestigious group. His photographic projects are often long-form investigative pieces covering sites of political conflict including Ukraine, Nicaragua, El Salvador, Standing Rock, and Afghanistan. His early work included documenting the Contra war in Nicaragua and the civil war in El Salvador. From 2008 to 2011, he traveled five times to Afghanistan to photograph the social effects of the Afghan civil war.

Towell has published numerous books of photographs, poetry, and oral history, and has recorded several audio CDs of original poetry and songs. He works in both film and digital photography formats and lives in rural Lambton County, where he sharecrops a 75-acre farm with his wife Ann and their four children.
achievements: Only Canadian member of Magnum Photos (1988)
World Press Photo of the Year winner (1994)
Henri Cartier-Bresson Foundation Award (2003)
Prix Nadar winner for 'No Man's Land' (2005)
Paul de Hueck and Norman Walford Career Achievement Award (2011)

figures.name: David Gilmer Towell
figures.title: U.S. Representative
figures.roleDescription: American politician who served one term as U.S. representative from Nevada
figures.significance: Served a single term as a Republican U.S. representative from Nevada's at-large district from 1973-1975.
figures.biography:
David Gilmer Towell was born on June 9, 1937, in Bronxville, New York, to a Canadian-born mother and an English-born father. He attended Bronxville and New York City public schools before earning a B.A. in economics at University of the Pacific in Stockton, California, in 1960. He served in the Nevada Air National Guard from 1960 to 1966 and worked as a real estate broker by profession.

Towell's political career began with serving as chairman of the Douglas County Republican Central Committee and as a delegate to Nevada State Republican conventions in 1968, 1970, and 1972. He ran for the U.S. House of Representatives in 1972, initially expecting to face eight-term conservative incumbent Democrat Walter Baring. However, when Baring was defeated in the Democratic primary by the more liberal James Bilbray, Towell benefited from the divided opposition and won by a narrow margin, boosted by a late endorsement from Baring himself and Richard Nixon's national landslide.

In 1974, Towell ran for reelection but was soundly defeated by more conservative Democrat and former judge Jim Santini. He made another attempt at federal office in 1976, running for the U.S. Senate against incumbent Democrat Howard Cannon, but lost by an overwhelming margin. After his political career ended, he resumed his real estate business. Towell died of cancer on June 10, 2003, at Washoe County Medical Center in Reno.
achievements: U.S. Representative from Nevada (1973-1975)
Chairman of Douglas County Republican Central Committee

figures.name: Mike Towell
figures.title: Professional Boxer
figures.roleDescription: Scottish professional boxer who died from injuries sustained in a fight
figures.significance: Scottish professional boxer whose death from brain injuries sustained in a 2016 fight led to significant safety discussions in the boxing community.
figures.biography:
Mike Towell was born on September 12, 1991, in Dundee, Scotland. He pursued a career as a professional boxer and competed in scheduled championship fights. On September 29, 2016, Towell fought Dale Evans at the Radisson Blu Hotel in Glasgow, where he suffered severe head injuries after being TKO'd in the fifth round of the scheduled 12-round fight.

Following the fight, Towell was immediately taken to the Queen Elizabeth University Hospital, where doctors discovered serious bleeding to the brain. Despite medical efforts, he died on September 30, 2016, one day after the fight. It was later reported that Towell had stopped sparring a few weeks before the fatal fight due to suffering from crippling migraines, which may have indicated underlying health issues.

Towell's death prompted an outpouring of support from the boxing community, with many fighters and promoters expressing condolences on social media. His funeral took place at St Andrew's Cathedral in Dundee on October 14, 2016. The boxing community raised significant funds for his family, and his story became the subject of Pete Carvill's book 'Death of a Boxer.' His death also sparked important conversations about boxer safety and medical protocols in professional boxing.

figures.name: Khimji Poonja
figures.title: Company Founder
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Khimji Poonja Freight Forwarders
figures.significance: Founded Khimji Poonja Freight Forwarders Pvt. Ltd. in 1922, establishing a successful customs and freight forwarding business in Mumbai.
figures.biography:
Khimji Poonja founded Khimji Poonja Freight Forwarders Pvt. Ltd. in 1922, establishing the company as a custom house agency and freight forwarding business based in Mumbai, India. Initially, the company dealt in cotton, oil seeds, and bullion. In 1946, he established a clearing and forwarding department which became one of the leading contributors to the company's activities.

The company he founded provides customs clearing services along with other allied logistics including air and sea freight forwarding, surface transport, insurance, warehousing and door-to-door delivery of goods. Under his establishment, the company grew to become an established player in the Indian logistics and freight forwarding industry, with various professional affiliations including IATA and FIATA membership.
achievements: Founded Khimji Poonja Freight Forwarders Pvt. Ltd. in 1922
Established clearing and forwarding department in 1946

figures.name: Lakhmidas Khimji
figures.title: Witness in Maharaj Libel Case
figures.roleDescription: Key witness in the famous 1862 Maharaj Libel Case
figures.significance: Served as a crucial witness in the landmark 1862 Maharaj Libel Case, providing testimony that influenced the court's decision.
figures.biography:
Lakhmidas Khimji was a significant witness in the famous Maharaj Libel Case of 1862 tried in the Supreme Court of Bombay. He provided crucial testimony during the defense's case, confirming other witness testimonies about alleged misconduct by religious leaders. His testimony was particularly notable as he claimed to have observed inappropriate behavior by Jadunath Maharaj.

Khimji's testimony was part of the broader case that stemmed from accusations published by reformist Karsandas Mulji against the Vallabhacharya and Pushtimarg Sect. The case became one of the most extraordinary trials in British India, receiving daily media coverage both in India and Europe. His role as a witness contributed to what became a landmark legal case that exposed divisions within the religious community.
achievements: Key witness in the landmark 1862 Maharaj Libel Case
